> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently lose 2–3 marks by (i) confusing A-mode and M-mode display axes, (ii) forgetting the speed of sound constant $c = 1540$ m/s when calculating depth, and (iii) failing to identify the D, E, F, A, C points of the mitral M-mode pattern by name. Always label these explicitly in diagrams.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023, Module 1]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between maximum imaging depth and Pulse Repetition Frequency in M-mode imaging. If PRF = 2 kHz, calculate the maximum depth. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation (4 marks):**
The transducer must wait for the echo from the deepest reflector to return before transmitting the next pulse. The round-trip time for a depth $D$ is:

$$t_r = \frac{2D}{c}$$

Therefore the minimum pulse interval is $T_{\min} = 2D/c$, and the maximum PRF is:

$$PRF_{\max} = \frac{1}{T_{\min}} = \frac{c}{2D_{\max}}$$

Rearranging for maximum depth:

$$D_{\max} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot PRF}$$

**Calculation (2 marks):** With $c = 1540$ m/s and $PRF = 2000$ Hz:

$$D_{\max} = \frac{1540}{2 \times 2000} = \frac{1540}{4000} = 0.385 \text{ m} = 38.5 \text{ cm}$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** 38.5 cm is more than sufficient to image the entire adult heart (typically 15–20 cm from chest wall).

**(b)** What is Fractional Shortening (FS) and how is it obtained from M-mode measurements? A patient has LVIDd = 5.2 cm and LVIDs = 3.4 cm. Calculate FS and comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Fractional Shortening (FS) is the percentage reduction in the left ventricular internal diameter (LVID) from end-diastole to end-systole, used as a measure of LV systolic contractile function.

**M-mode measurement (2 marks):** From a parasternal long-axis view, the M-mode cursor is placed perpendicular to the LV at the level of the chordae tendineae (just below the mitral valve tips). The end-diastolic and end-systolic LVIDs are measured using digital calipers on the M-mode tracing.

**Calculation (2 marks):**

$$FS = \frac{LVID_d - LVID_s}{LVID_d} \times 100\% = \frac{5.2 - 3.4}{5.2} \times 100\% = \frac{1.8}{5.2} \times 100\% \approx 34.6\%$$

**Comment (1 mark):** A fractional shortening of ~35% lies well within the **normal range of 25–45%**, indicating **normal left ventricular contractility**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students incorrectly use the M-mode derived diameter to *directly* compute ejection fraction via simple geometric formulas, ignoring the Teichholz correction for non-spherical LV geometry. Always state the assumptions (Teichholz, cube, or Simpson) explicitly. Also, do not mix units — LVIDd and LVIDs must be in the *same* unit (cm *or* mm) before substitution.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **M-mode = Motion-mode** = 1-D time-motion ultrasound with single fixed scan line.
- **Display axes:** Y-axis = **Depth** (cm); X-axis = **Time** (s); Brightness = echo amplitude.
- **Speed of sound in soft tissue:** $c = 1540$ m/s (use this exact value unless otherwise specified).
- **Depth formula:** $d = c \cdot t_r / 2$ — always remember the factor of **2** for round-trip.
- **Max depth vs PRF:** $D_{\max} = c / (2 \cdot PRF)$ — higher PRF → shallower maximum depth.
- **Axial resolution:** $\Delta z = c / (2B)$ — set by pulse bandwidth $B$, not by frequency alone.
- **Typical M-mode PRF:** ~1000 Hz → **1 ms temporal resolution** (the key advantage of M-mode).
- **Mitral valve M-mode landmarks (AML):** D, E, F, A, C points; **CD segment** = closed valve in systole.
- **PML motion:** mirror image of AML (forms "W" pattern).
- **Fractional Shortening (FS):** $FS = (LVID_d - LVID_s)/LVID_d \times 100\%$; normal = **25–45 %**.
- **Ejection Fraction (Teichholz):** $EF = (EDV - ESV)/EDV \times 100\%$; normal = **55–70 %**.
- **Clinical signatures to recognize:**
  - Mitral stenosis → reduced E-F slope.
  - MVP → mid-systolic posterior bowing of CD.
  - HOCM → Systolic Anterior Motion (SAM) of AML.
  - Aortic regurgitation → high-frequency fluttering of AML.
  - Pericardial effusion → echo-free space posterior to LV.
- **Standard M-mode LV measurements (parasternal, chordae level):** LVIDd, LVIDs, IVS, PW.
- **Why M-mode is still relevant:** Unmatched **temporal resolution**, simplicity, and quantitative accuracy for wall thickness, chamber dimension, and valve timing — even in the era of 2D/3D/Doppler imaging.
- **Embedded in 2D:** Modern M-mode is displayed by streaming one line from a 2D B-mode sector, with the operator placing the M-mode cursor on the target.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** When asked to "explain M-mode echocardiography" in long-answer form, always cover: (1) principle (pulse-echo, single fixed line), (2) display format (Y-depth, X-time, B-amplitude), (3) clinical applications (measurements + valve motion), (4) one worked numerical problem (depth or FS), and (5) a labelled mitral M-mode diagram. This is the exact structure KTU board examiners look for to award full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: B-mode
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT721 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** B-mode

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**B-mode (Brightness Mode) imaging** is a two-dimensional (2D), real-time, grayscale ultrasonographic display modality in which the **intensity (luminance) of each pixel** is modulated by the **amplitude of the returning ultrasound echo** from the corresponding spatial location within the scanned tissue plane. The horizontal axis of the display encodes the **lateral (azimuthal) position** of the scan line, while the vertical axis encodes the **axial (depth) position**, derived from the time-of-flight (TOF) of the reflected acoustic pulse.

Formally, for a scan-converted B-mode image $I(x, y, t)$:

$$I(x, y, t) = g\big[A(\tau, \theta, t)\big]$$

where $A(\tau, \theta, t)$ is the envelope-detected echo amplitude at axial time delay $\tau = 2z/c$ and lateral steering angle $\theta$, and $g(\cdot)$ is the post-processing (logarithmic compression and mapping) function. The axial resolution is governed by the **spatial pulse length (SPL)**, while the lateral resolution is governed by the **beamwidth** at depth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** B-mode is treated as the foundational 2D extension of A-mode. Examiners expect students to articulate the *scan conversion* from polar $(r, \theta)$ to Cartesian $(x, y)$ coordinates, the role of the **envelope detector**, and the **dynamic range compression** (typically **60 dB** displayed on a **40–50 dB** monitor range) as core assessment points.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note:** B-mode was pioneered in the early 1950s by **Douglas Howry**, **Joseph Holmes**, and **John Wild**, evolving from the static compound B-scanner (water-bath immersion) to the modern real-time electronic scanner using **phased-array** and **linear-array** transducers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a dark room and you shout a single word: *"Echo!"* The sound waves travel outward, hit the walls, furniture, and objects, and bounce back to your ears. Now imagine you have a **superpower** that lets you:
1. **Time** exactly how long each echo takes to return → this tells you **how far** the object is (depth).
2. **Listen to how loud** each returning echo is → this tells you **how dense or reflective** the object is (brightness).
3. **Sweep** your voice across the room from left to right → this builds up a **2D mental map** of the room.

B-mode ultrasound does **exactly this** with **high-frequency sound (2–15 MHz)** inside the human body. The transducer is your "mouth and ears," the scanner is your "brain," and the monitor is your "mental map." A bright pixel = strong reflector (e.g., bone, organ boundary); a dark pixel = weak reflector (e.g., clear fluid in the bladder).

> [!TIP]
> **Student Mnemonic — "**B** for **B**rightness, **B**uilding blocks of ultrasound":**
> - **B**rightness $\propto$ Echo **A**mplitude (envelope of RF signal)
> - **B**uilds 2D image from 1D A-lines (scan conversion)
> - **B**andwidth of transducer $\to$ axial resolution

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Speed of sound in soft tissue ($c$) | **1540** | m/s |
| Diagnostic frequency range ($f_0$) | **2 – 15** | MHz |
| Wavelength ($\lambda = c/f_0$) | **0.10 – 0.77** | mm |
| Axial resolution (≈ SPL/2) | **0.2 – 1.0** | mm |
| Lateral resolution (≈ beamwidth) | **0.5 – 3.0** | mm |
| Dynamic range (echo RF) | **~100 – 120** | dB |
| Displayed dynamic range | **~40 – 60** | dB |
| Typical frame rate (cardiac) | **30 – 60** | fps |
| Typical frame rate (abdominal) | **15 – 30** | fps |
| Number of scan lines per frame | **64 – 256** | lines |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — From Pulse to Pixel

The B-mode image formation pipeline can be decomposed into **seven sequential stages**. Each stage contributes to the final image quality and is a potential KTU question.

### Stage 1 — Pulse Generation (Transmit Beamforming)
- The system generates a **short, high-voltage electrical pulse** (typically $\pm$ 50–100 V, 1–3 cycles) at the **center frequency $f_0$**.
- The pulse is applied (with calibrated **delays** for phased arrays) across the transducer elements to produce a focused transmit beam.
- The **spatial pulse length (SPL)** is given by:

$$\text{SPL} = n \cdot \lambda$$

where $n$ is the number of cycles in the pulse. For $n=2$ and $f_0 = 5\,\text{MHz}$:

$$\text{SPL} = 2 \times \frac{1540}{5 \times 10^{6}} = 0.616\;\text{mm}$$

### Stage 2 — Echo Reception (Receive Beamforming)
- Returning echoes arrive at each element with different time delays (curved wavefront).
- **Dynamic receive focusing** applies element-specific time delays to align the wavefronts coherently.
- The aligned signals are **summed** to form one **A-line** (radiofrequency, RF).

### Stage 3 — Time-Gain Compensation (TGC)
- Deeper echoes are weaker due to **attenuation** $\alpha$ (in dB/cm/MHz):

$$A(z) = A_0 \cdot 10^{-\alpha z f_0 / 20}$$

- For soft tissue, $\alpha \approx 0.5\text{–}1.0\;\text{dB/cm/MHz}$.
- TGC amplifies deeper signals to produce a **visually uniform** image.

### Stage 4 — Envelope Detection
- The RF A-line is **rectified** (absolute value) and **low-pass filtered** to extract the envelope $A(\tau)$.
- This converts the oscillating RF signal into a slowly varying **amplitude profile**.

### Stage 5 — Logarithmic Compression
- The dynamic range of $A(\tau)$ (~100 dB) is **compressed** to display range (~50 dB) via:

$$A_{\text{log}}(\tau) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left[\frac{A(\tau)}{A_{\text{ref}}}\right]$$

### Stage 6 — Scan Conversion
- A-lines are acquired in **polar coordinates** $(r, \theta)$; the monitor displays in **Cartesian** $(x, y)$.
- Transformation:

$$x = r \cos\theta,\quad y = r \sin\theta$$

- Most modern systems use a **digital scan converter (DSC)** with frame memory.

### Stage 7 — Display
- The compressed values are mapped to **grayscale levels** (8-bit = 256 shades) and displayed in real time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Why" Matters:** The KTU examiner often asks: *"Why is logarithmic compression necessary?"* Answer: Human visual perception and CRT/LCD displays can only reproduce a dynamic range of **~30–50 dB** (about **6–9 bits**), but raw echoes span **100+ dB**. Log compression maps this wide range to the displayable range.

## 2.2 Beam Geometry & Resolution

| Quantity | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Axial resolution | $\delta_a \approx \frac{\text{SPL}}{2} = \frac{n \lambda}{2}$ | Smaller $\lambda$ (higher $f$) → better |
| Lateral resolution (focal zone) | $\delta_l \approx 1.22 \frac{\lambda F}{D}$ | $F$ = focal depth, $D$ = aperture |
| Depth of field (DOF) | $\text{DOF} \approx 7 \lambda \left(\frac{F}{D}\right)^{2}$ | Trade-off with $\delta_l$ |
| Pulse repetition frequency (PRF) | $\text{PRF} \leq \frac{c}{2 z_{\text{max}}}$ | For unambiguous ranging |
| Frame rate (FR) | $\text{FR} = \frac{\text{PRF}}{N}$ | $N$ = number of scan lines |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (One-Page Revision)

| # | Formula | Meaning / Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $z = \frac{c \tau}{2}$ | Depth from round-trip TOF; the cornerstone of pulse-echo ranging |
| 2 | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f_0}$ | Wavelength governs **all** resolution limits |
| 3 | $\text{SPL} = n \lambda$ | Spatial pulse length; sets axial resolution |
| 4 | $\delta_a = \dfrac{n \lambda}{2}$ | Axial resolution; clinically the most important |
| 5 | $\delta_l = 1.22 \dfrac{\lambda F}{D}$ | Lateral resolution at focal depth $F$ |
| 6 | $\text{PRF}_{\max} = \dfrac{c}{2 z_{\max}}$ | Maximum unambiguous PRF; ties depth to frame rate |
| 7 | $\text{FR} = \dfrac{\text{PRF}}{N}$ | Real-time frame rate; cardiac needs FR $\geq 30$ fps |
| 8 | $A(z) = A_0 \cdot 10^{-\alpha z f_0 / 20}$ | Attenuation in tissue; motivates TGC |
| 9 | $A_{\text{log}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A}{A_{\text{ref}}}\right)$ | Logarithmic compression; maps dB to grayscale |
| 10 | $x = r\cos\theta,\; y = r\sin\theta$ | Polar-to-Cartesian scan conversion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** B-mode is the workhorse of **radiology, obstetrics, cardiology, and vascular imaging**. Real-time guidance enables **biopsy needle tracking, fetal monitoring, and emergency FAST scans**. The same scan-conversion principle underlies **synthetic aperture radar (SAR)** and **sonar bathymetry**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Depth from Time-of-Flight (TOF)

**Given:** A pulse is transmitted at $t=0$, reflects from an interface at depth $z$, and the echo returns at time $t = \tau$. The speed of sound in soft tissue is $c = 1540\;\text{m/s}$.

**To derive:** The depth $z$ as a function of $\tau$.

**Step 1 —** The pulse travels **down** to the reflector over a one-way distance $z$ in time $z/c$.

$$t_{\text{down}} = \frac{z}{c}$$

**Step 2 —** The reflected echo travels **back up** the same distance in the same time.

$$t_{\text{up}} = \frac{z}{c}$$

**Step 3 —** The total round-trip time is the sum:

$$\tau = t_{\text{down}} + t_{\text{up}} = \frac{z}{c} + \frac{z}{c} = \frac{2z}{c}$$

**Step 4 —** Solve for $z$:

$$z = \frac{c\, \tau}{2}$$

**Numerical Example:** If an echo is detected at $\tau = 26\;\mu\text{s}$:

$$z = \frac{1540 \times 26 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = \frac{0.04004}{2} = 0.02002\;\text{m} \approx 20\;\text{mm}$$

The interface is **20 mm deep**.

> [!NOTE]
> The factor of **2** in the denominator is the mathematical fingerprint of *all* pulse-echo (active) ranging systems — radar, sonar, ultrasound, and LiDAR.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF)

**Given:** Maximum imaging depth $z_{\max}$. The transducer must "wait" for the deepest echo to return before transmitting the next pulse (to avoid **range ambiguity**).

**Step 1 —** Round-trip time to depth $z_{\max}$:

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{2 z_{\max}}{c}$$

**Step 2 —** PRF is the reciprocal of the minimum pulse-to-pulse interval $\tau_{\max}$:

$$\text{PRF}_{\max} = \frac{1}{\tau_{\max}} = \frac{c}{2 z_{\max}}$$

**Numerical Example:** For $z_{\max} = 15\;\text{cm} = 0.15\;\text{m}$:

$$\text{PRF}_{\max} = \frac{1540}{2 \times 0.15} = \frac{1540}{0.30} \approx 5133\;\text{Hz} \approx 5.13\;\text{kHz}$$

For a frame with $N = 128$ scan lines:

$$\text{FR} = \frac{\text{PRF}_{\max}}{N} = \frac{5133}{128} \approx 40.1\;\text{fps}$$

This is sufficient for **real-time cardiac imaging**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Lateral Resolution at the Focal Point

**Given:** A circular aperture of diameter $D$ produces a focused beam with first-minimum radius (Airy disk analog) at focal depth $F$.

**Step 1 —** By analogy with optical diffraction, the half-angle of the first minimum is:

$$\sin\phi \approx 1.22 \frac{\lambda}{D}$$

**Step 2 —** The lateral (full) beamwidth at the focal plane is:

$$\delta_l = 2 F \sin\phi \approx 2 F \cdot 1.22 \frac{\lambda}{D} = 1.22 \frac{\lambda F}{D}$$

**Numerical Example:** $f_0 = 5\;\text{MHz},\; \lambda = 0.308\;\text{mm},\; F = 50\;\text{mm},\; D = 20\;\text{mm}$:

$$\delta_l = 1.22 \times \frac{0.308 \times 50}{20} = 1.22 \times 0.77 = 0.94\;\text{mm}$$

The lateral resolution at focus is **≈ 0.94 mm** — comparable to axial resolution, as desired.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Attenuation & TGC Required Gain

**Given:** Attenuation coefficient $\alpha = 0.7\;\text{dB/cm/MHz}$ in liver tissue; imaging depth $z = 10\;\text{cm}$; transmit frequency $f_0 = 4\;\text{MHz}$.

**Step 1 —** Total one-way attenuation in dB:

$$A_{\text{dB}} = \alpha \cdot z \cdot f_0 = 0.7 \times 10 \times 4 = 28\;\text{dB}$$

**Step 2 —** Round-trip attenuation:

$$A_{\text{rt}} = 2 \times 28 = 56\;\text{dB}$$

**Step 3 —** Linear amplification factor required:

$$G_{\text{lin}} = 10^{56/20} = 10^{2.8} \approx 631$$

The TGC amplifier must provide a **~631× (56 dB) gain** at 10 cm depth to compensate.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Parameter Trade-offs

| If you increase... | Axial resolution | Lateral resolution | Penetration | Frame rate |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency $f_0$ | **Improves** ($\downarrow \lambda$) | **Improves** | **Degrades** ($\uparrow \alpha$) | Unchanged directly |
| Aperture $D$ | Unchanged | **Improves** ($\downarrow \delta_l$) | Unchanged | Unchanged |
| Number of cycles $n$ | **Degrades** ($\uparrow$ SPL) | Unchanged | Unchanged | Unchanged |
| Number of lines $N$ | Unchanged | Unchanged | Unchanged | **Degrades** |
| Depth $z_{\max}$ | Unchanged | Degrades (far field) | — | **Degrades** |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Strategy:** When asked *"Why don't we just use 50 MHz for everything?"*, the answer is a **two-part trade-off**: (1) attenuation rises linearly with frequency ($\alpha z f_0$), so penetration collapses; (2) at very high frequency, the mechanical/thermal index (MI/TI) safety limits are approached.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a B-Mode Scanner

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete signal chain from the transducer to the displayed grayscale image.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Master Clock and PRF Generator] --> P1[Transmit Beamformer Delays]
    P1 --> P2[Pulsed HV Excitation 2 to 3 cycles]
    P2 --> P3[Transducer Array Piezoelectric]
    P3 --> P4[Patient Tissue Acoustic Path]
    P4 --> P5[Receiving Echoes at Array]
    P5 --> P6[Receive Beamformer Dynamic Focus TGC]
    P6 --> P7[RF A Line 1D Signal]
    P7 --> P8[Envelope Detector Rectify plus LPF]
    P8 --> P9[Log Compression 100 dB to 50 dB]
    P9 --> P10[Scan Converter Polar to Cartesian]
    P10 --> P11[Frame Memory 8 bit]
    P11 --> P12[Grayscale LUT Mapping]
    P12 --> P13[Monitor Display Real Time]
    P8 --> P14[Speckle Reduction Filter Optional]
    P14 --> P9
    P6 --> P15[Rejection Filter Clutter]
    P15 --> P8
```

## 4.2 Scan Conversion Geometry — Polar to Cartesian

The transducer is located at the **apex** of a sector image (phased array) or at the top of a rectangular image (linear array). The following diagram shows the geometry and the pixel mapping.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POLAR_ACQ ["Polar Acquisition Space"]
        R0[Origin Transducer]
        A0[A line angle theta 1]
        A1[A line angle theta 2]
        A2[A line angle theta N]
        A3[Depth axis r along A line]
    end
    subgraph CART_DISP ["Cartesian Display Space"]
        C0[Origin at top center]
        C1[X axis lateral]
        C2[Y axis depth]
    end
    R0 --> A0
    R0 --> A1
    R0 --> A2
    A0 --> A3
    A3 -->|interpolation x=r cos theta y=r sin theta| C1
    C1 --> C2
    C0 --- C1
    C0 --- C2
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Echogram — A-Mode vs B-Mode

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph A_MODE ["A mode 1D Plot"]
        AM1[Time axis x]
        AM2[Amplitude axis y spikes]
    end
    subgraph B_MODE ["B mode 2D Image"]
        BM1[Scan line index x lateral]
        BM2[Depth axis y]
        BM3[Brightness z echo amplitude]
    end
    AM1 -->|temporal sequential scanning| BM1
    AM2 -->|brightness modulation of pixels| BM3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Trace the signal flow in §4.1 from top to bottom. The *two parallel side branches* (Speckle Filter and Clutter Rejection) are **optional but clinically crucial** post-processing stages — often asked about in KTU Part B questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Differentiate between A-mode and B-mode ultrasound imaging. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Model Answer (≈ 90 words):**

| Aspect | A-mode | B-mode |
|---|---|---|
| Dimensions | 1-D (oscilloscope trace) | 2-D (grayscale image) |
| Display | Amplitude vs. time spikes | Brightness vs. (x, y) pixels |
| Information | Echo amplitude only | Amplitude + spatial position |
| Use | Ophthalmology, echoencephalography | General radiology, OB/GYN, cardiac |
| Output | Waveform | Real-time anatomical image |

In **A-mode**, echo strength is plotted as vertical deflection; in **B-mode**, the same echo strength modulates the **brightness (luminance)** of a pixel located at the corresponding depth and lateral position, producing a **2D cross-sectional image** of tissue.

### Q2. What is the role of logarithmic compression in a B-mode scanner? [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Model Answer (≈ 75 words):**

The detected echo envelope has a dynamic range of **~100 dB** (factor of $10^5$ in amplitude), but display devices (CRT/LCD) and the human eye can only resolve **~40–50 dB** of grayscale (≈ 256 shades, 8-bit). **Logarithmic compression** maps the wide input range to the displayable range using $A_{\log} = 20 \log_{10}(A/A_{\text{ref}})$, producing a **visually balanced** image where both strong and weak reflectors are simultaneously discernible.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A** [14 Marks] — B-Mode Image Formation Pipeline

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **complete B-mode image formation pipeline** from pulse generation to display. Explain the function of each block. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Derive the expression for **depth of an interface from the round-trip time of flight**. An ultrasound system operating at 5 MHz detects an echo at 39 µs. Calculate the depth of the reflecting interface. Take $c = 1540\;\text{m/s}$. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The B-mode image formation pipeline consists of the following sequential blocks (refer to §4.1):

1. **Master Clock & PRF Generator:** Sets the timing of pulse transmission; PRF $\leq c/(2z_{\max})$.
2. **Transmit Beamformer:** Applies calibrated electronic **delays** to each element of the array to **steer and focus** the transmit beam.
3. **HV Pulse Excitation:** Applies a **short, high-voltage pulse** (50–100 V, 2–3 cycles) at $f_0$ to the elements.
4. **Transducer Array:** Converts electrical energy to **acoustic** energy via the **piezoelectric effect** (inverse); on receive, converts echoes back to electrical signals (direct).
5. **Receive Beamformer & TGC:** Aligns returning echoes with element-specific delays (dynamic focus) and applies **time-gain compensation** to compensate for depth-dependent attenuation.
6. **Clutter/Rejection Filter:** Removes low-frequency motion artifacts from vessel walls and breathing.
7. **Envelope Detector:** Rectifies and low-pass filters the RF A-line to extract the **envelope** (slowly varying amplitude).
8. **Speckle Reduction Filter (optional):** Reduces multiplicative speckle noise using techniques like Lee, Frost, or SRAD filters.
9. **Logarithmic Compression:** Maps the **~100 dB** echo dynamic range to the **~50 dB** display range.
10. **Scan Converter:** Transforms the polar-coordinate data $(r, \theta)$ to Cartesian $(x, y)$ using $x = r\cos\theta,\; y = r\sin\theta$ via bilinear or nearest-neighbor interpolation.
11. **Frame Memory & LUT:** Stores the **8-bit** pixel matrix; the **look-up table** maps the compressed values to grayscale intensities.
12. **Display:** Renders the image in real time at the calculated frame rate.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Derivation (refer to §3.1):**

$$\tau = t_{\text{down}} + t_{\text{up}} = \frac{z}{c} + \frac{z}{c} = \frac{2z}{c}$$

$$\boxed{\,z = \frac{c\,\tau}{2}\,}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

$$z = \frac{1540 \times 39 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = \frac{0.06006}{2} = 0.03003\;\text{m}$$

$$\boxed{\,z \approx 30.0\;\text{mm} = 3.0\;\text{cm}\,}$$

The reflecting interface lies at a depth of **30 mm**.

---

### **Question B** [14 Marks] — Resolution, PRF & Frame Rate

**(a)** Explain **axial and lateral resolution** in B-mode imaging. Derive expressions for both and discuss the trade-off with frequency and aperture. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A B-mode scanner images tissue to a maximum depth of **18 cm** with **192 scan lines per frame**. Calculate the **maximum PRF** and the resulting **frame rate**. Take $c = 1540\;\text{m/s}$. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Axial Resolution** is the ability to distinguish two reflectors lying **along the beam axis**. It is governed by the **spatial pulse length (SPL)**:

$$\delta_a \approx \frac{\text{SPL}}{2} = \frac{n \lambda}{2} = \frac{n c}{2 f_0}$$

Two reflectors separated by less than $\delta_a$ produce **overlapping echoes** and appear as a single structure. **Higher frequency** $f_0$ and **fewer cycles** $n$ improve axial resolution.

**Lateral Resolution** is the ability to distinguish two reflectors lying **perpendicular to the beam axis** at the same depth. It equals the **beamwidth** at that depth, which at the focal point is:

$$\delta_l \approx 1.22 \frac{\lambda F}{D} = 1.22 \frac{c F}{f_0 D}$$

**Trade-offs:**
- Increasing $f_0$ improves **both** resolutions but **reduces penetration** (attenuation $\propto f_0$).
- Increasing aperture $D$ improves lateral resolution but reduces **depth of field** (DOF) and increases **side-lobe** levels.
- Decreasing $n$ (shorter pulse) improves axial resolution but **decreases SNR** (less energy per pulse).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Maximum PRF** (refer to §3.2):

$$\text{PRF}_{\max} = \frac{c}{2 z_{\max}} = \frac{1540}{2 \times 0.18} = \frac{1540}{0.36}$$

$$\boxed{\,\text{PRF}_{\max} \approx 4278\;\text{Hz} \approx 4.28\;\text{kHz}\,}$$

**Step 2 — Frame Rate:**

$$\text{FR} = \frac{\text{PRF}_{\max}}{N} = \frac{4278}{192}$$

$$\boxed{\,\text{FR} \approx 22.3\;\text{fps}\,}$$

This frame rate is suitable for **abdominal and obstetric** imaging but marginal for high-speed cardiac applications (where line density or depth must be reduced).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in TOF:** Many students write $z = c\tau$ instead of $z = c\tau/2$. The factor of 2 comes from the **round-trip** travel — this is the **#1 most common error** in KTU papers.
> 2. **Confusing axial and lateral resolution:** Axial = along the beam (SPL); lateral = across the beam (beamwidth). Mixing them up costs full marks.
> 3. **Ignoring units:** Always convert µs to seconds, mm to m, before substitution. KTU examiners specifically look for unit consistency.
> 4. **Not mentioning the Nyquist/sampling link:** PRF is tied to **range ambiguity**, not just to frame rate. State this explicitly.
> 5. **Omitting the scan-conversion step:** A B-mode answer that does not mention polar → Cartesian conversion is considered *incomplete* under KTU 2024 rubric.
> 6. **Dynamic range units:** Dynamic range is in **dB**, not a dimensionless ratio. State this in the answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **B-mode = Brightness mode** — 2D grayscale image where pixel intensity = echo amplitude.
- Built on the **pulse-echo principle**: transmit → reflect → receive → display.
- **Depth** is computed from **round-trip time-of-flight**: $z = c\tau/2$ with $c = 1540\;\text{m/s}$.
- **Axial resolution** = $\text{SPL}/2 = n\lambda/2 = nc/(2f_0)$ — improves with **higher $f_0$**.
- **Lateral resolution** = $1.22\lambda F/D$ — improves with **higher $f_0$ and larger $D$**.
- **PRF** is limited by the **deepest unambiguous range**: $\text{PRF}_{\max} = c/(2 z_{\max})$.
- **Frame rate** = PRF / N (number of scan lines per frame) — typical **15–60 fps**.
- **Log compression** is mandatory because echo dynamic range (~100 dB) ≫ display range (~50 dB).
- **Scan conversion** transforms polar $(r, \theta)$ data to Cartesian $(x, y)$ display.
- **TGC** compensates for depth-dependent attenuation $\alpha z f_0$.
- **Speckle** is a multiplicative noise inherent to coherent imaging; it is *not* a true anatomical feature.
- **Trade-off mantra:** *Higher frequency = better resolution but less penetration.*
- B-mode is the foundation of all modern real-time ultrasound; Doppler, M-mode, and elastography are extensions built on top of it.
- Examiner keywords to use: **"round-trip TOF," "spatial pulse length," "dynamic range compression," "scan conversion," "time-gain compensation," "beamwidth-limited lateral resolution."**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of Doppler ultrasonic imaging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT721 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Principles of Doppler ultrasonic imaging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Doppler Ultrasonic Imaging** is a specialized diagnostic ultrasound modality that exploits the **Doppler Effect** — the apparent change in frequency of a wave when there is relative motion between the source and the observer — to non-invasively detect, quantify, and visualize the motion of internal body tissues, primarily **red blood cells within the cardiovascular system**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Doppler ultrasound is classified as a *functional imaging* modality, in contrast to conventional B-mode (Brightness mode) ultrasound which provides *anatomical* imaging. The fundamental shift in the received ultrasound frequency (called the **Doppler shift**, denoted **Δf** or **f_d**) is directly proportional to the velocity of the moving reflectors (erythrocytes) and forms the quantitative basis for estimating blood flow velocity, volume flow rate, and turbulence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under Module 1 of OEBRT721, students are expected to derive the Doppler equation, distinguish between Continuous Wave (CW) and Pulsed Wave (PW) Doppler, and analyze the principles of Color Flow Mapping (CFM).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Ambulance Siren Effect"

Imagine standing on a sidewalk as an ambulance with a blaring siren approaches you. As the ambulance moves **toward** you, each successive sound wave is emitted from a position *closer* to your ears, which **compresses** the waves and raises the perceived pitch (frequency). As the ambulance passes and moves **away**, the waves are stretched, and the pitch drops. This frequency change is the **Doppler Effect**.

In Doppler ultrasound, the ambulance is the **transmitting ultrasound transducer**, the medium (air) is replaced by **soft tissue and blood**, and your ears are replaced by the **receiving transducer**. However, in medical Doppler, the ultrasound source and receiver are typically *stationary*, and the **reflectors (red blood cells) are moving**. This is the *passive* Doppler configuration. The shift is extremely small — typically **100 Hz to 10 kHz** — compared to the transmitted frequency of **2 to 10 MHz**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Speed of ultrasound in soft tissue | **c** | **1540 m/s** |
| Transmitted frequency | **f₀** | **2 – 10 MHz** |
| Speed of sound in blood | **c_b** | **1570 m/s** |
| Blood velocity range | **v** | **0.1 – 5 m/s** |
| Angle of insonation | **θ** | **0° – 90°** (optimal 30°–60°) |
| Doppler shift frequency | **f_d** | **100 Hz – 10 kHz** |
| Pulse Repetition Frequency | **PRF** | **1 – 25 kHz** |

> [!NOTE]
> The standard convention **c = 1540 m/s** is used universally in clinical ultrasound systems as the average propagation speed through human soft tissue at body temperature (37 °C). This is calibrated by the American Institute of Ultrasound in Medicine (AIUM) and is the value used in all KTU derivations.

## 1.4 Historical and Clinical Context

The principle was first described by Austrian physicist **Christian Johann Doppler** in **1842**. Its application to medical ultrasound was pioneered by **Satomura (1957)** and **Baker (1970)**, leading to the modern duplex and color Doppler systems used today in cardiology, obstetrics, and vascular surgery.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Doppler Equation — Step-by-Step Logic

The Doppler shift detected by an ultrasound system arises from a **two-stage** frequency change:

1. **Stage 1 (Moving Reflector as Observer):** The red blood cell, moving with velocity **v** at an angle **θ** to the ultrasound beam, perceives the transmitted wave at a frequency **f'** that is *higher* than **f₀** if it moves *toward* the source.

2. **Stage 2 (Reflector as Source):** The blood cell then re-radiates (back-scatters) the wave at **f'**, and the stationary transducer receives it at a still-different frequency, completing the round-trip shift.

### Why Two Stages?

Because the ultrasound wave must travel **to the moving target** and the reflected wave must travel **back from the moving target** to the transducer, the Doppler shift is effectively **doubled** compared to a one-way passive observation. This is the origin of the factor **"2"** in the medical Doppler equation.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **complete formula set** you must memorize for KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE) on this topic.

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_d = \dfrac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$ | General Doppler shift equation | Hz |
| 2 | $v = \dfrac{f_d c}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}$ | Velocity of blood flow | m/s |
| 3 | $\theta = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{f_d c}{2 f_0 v}\right)$ | Angle of insonation | degrees |
| 4 | $v_{max} = \dfrac{c}{4 f_0 T_p} = \dfrac{c \cdot \text{PRF}}{4 f_0}$ | Maximum unambiguous velocity (PW Doppler, Nyquist limit) | m/s |
| 5 | $f_{Nyquist} = \dfrac{\text{PRF}}{2}$ | Nyquist frequency (sampling limit) | Hz |
| 6 | $Q = v \cdot A$ | Volume flow rate (Q) | m³/s |
| 7 | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number (for turbulence detection) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{Standoff} = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | Depth of sample volume | m |

Where:
- $f_d$ = Doppler shift frequency (Hz)
- $f_0$ = Transmitted (carrier) ultrasound frequency (Hz)
- $v$ = Velocity of blood cells (m/s)
- $\theta$ = Angle between ultrasound beam and direction of blood flow (degrees)
- $c$ = Speed of sound in tissue = **1540 m/s**
- $\text{PRF}$ = Pulse Repetition Frequency (Hz)
- $T_p$ = Pulse repetition period (s) = $1/\text{PRF}$
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area of vessel (m²)
- $\rho$ = Blood density = **1060 kg/m³**
- $\mu$ = Blood viscosity ≈ **3–4 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**
- $D$ = Vessel diameter (m)
- $\Delta t$ = Time between pulse transmission and echo reception (s)

## 2.3 Physical Interpretation of the Angle θ

The term **cos θ** in the Doppler equation is critical. Notice the following:

- When **θ = 0°** (beam aligned with flow): $\cos\theta = 1$, **maximum sensitivity**. This is the ideal case.
- When **θ = 90°** (beam perpendicular to flow): $\cos\theta = 0$, **no Doppler shift detected**, even if blood is moving rapidly.
- When **θ = 30°**: $\cos 30° = 0.866$ (about 13.4% error if not corrected).
- When **θ = 60°**: $\cos 60° = 0.500$ (50% underestimation if assumed parallel).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** A very common error is forgetting that the angle is between the **ultrasound beam** and the **flow direction**, not the vessel wall. In real vessels, the **flow direction is parallel to the vessel axis**, not perpendicular to the wall.

## 2.4 Continuous Wave (CW) vs. Pulsed Wave (PW) Doppler

The Doppler equation applies to *both* modes, but the implementation differs fundamentally:

| Feature | Continuous Wave (CW) | Pulsed Wave (PW) |
|---|---|---|
| Transducers | **Two** crystals (one Tx, one Rx) | **One** crystal (time-shared) |
| Transmitted wave | Continuous sinusoid | Short pulses |
| Range resolution (depth gating) | **None** (all flow along beam detected) | **Yes** (selectable sample volume) |
| Maximum measurable velocity | **Unlimited** (no aliasing) | Limited by **Nyquist** ($v_{max} = c \cdot \text{PRF}/4f_0$) |
| Clinical use | Cardiac valves (high velocities) | Standard vessel flow |
| Aliasing | Never occurs | **Common problem** at high velocities |

## 2.5 Color Flow Mapping (CFM) — Principles

Color Doppler extends pulsed Doppler by using **multiple sample volumes** (typically 4–16) along each scan line. The **mean velocity** and its **direction** at each location are encoded as:

- **Red** → flow **toward** the transducer
- **Blue** → flow **away from** the transducer
- **Brightness/Saturation** → magnitude of velocity
- **Green (variance map)** → turbulent flow

The autocorrelation processor computes the mean phase shift between successive pulses for each sample volume — this is what differentiates Color Doppler from simple spectral PW Doppler.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Doppler ultrasound is indispensable in:

- **Cardiology:** Detection of valve stenosis/regurgitation, shunt quantification.
- **Obstetrics:** Fetal heart rate monitoring, umbilical cord flow assessment.
- **Vascular Surgery:** Carotid stenosis detection, deep vein thrombosis (DVT).
- **Neurology:** Transcranial Doppler for cerebral blood flow.
- **Oncology:** Tumor vascularity characterization via power Doppler.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of the Doppler Equation

We will derive the equation:

$$f_d = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$$

from first principles using the **classical Doppler Effect**.

### Stage 1: The Transducer Transmits, the Blood Cell Receives

Let the transducer (Tx) be stationary. It emits a wave of frequency $f_0$ and wavelength $\lambda_0 = c/f_0$ into the tissue.

The blood cell is moving with velocity $v$ at an angle $\theta$ **to the beam axis** (note: only the component of velocity *along the beam* matters, which is $v \cos\theta$).

According to the classical Doppler effect, the frequency perceived by the moving observer (blood cell) is:

$$f' = f_0 \left( \frac{c - v\cos\theta}{c} \right)$$

This is because, as the cell moves *toward* the source, it intercepts wavefronts at a faster rate (numerator decreases by $v \cos\theta$).

### Stage 2: The Blood Cell Re-radiates, the Transducer Receives

Now the blood cell acts as a *moving source*, re-radiating the wave at frequency $f'$. Since the transducer (observer) is stationary, the received frequency $f_r$ is:

$$f_r = f' \left( \frac{c}{c - v\cos\theta} \right)$$

For a moving source approaching a stationary observer, the apparent frequency is the transmitted frequency multiplied by $c / (c - v_s)$, where $v_s$ is the source velocity component along the line of sight.

### Combining Both Stages

Substituting $f'$ into $f_r$:

$$f_r = f_0 \left( \frac{c - v\cos\theta}{c} \right) \cdot \left( \frac{c}{c - v\cos\theta} \right)$$

Wait — this would give $f_r = f_0$, which is wrong. The reason is that we have used the **same angle** in both stages. In reality, the back-scattered wave is reflected *toward* the transducer, and the angle for the **incoming** path is also $\theta$ but the sign reverses.

The correct application requires careful sign convention. Using the generalized Doppler formula with the angle $\theta$ between beam and flow, and recognizing that the cell moves **toward the Tx on the way out and toward the Tx on the way back** (when flow is toward Tx):

$$f_d = f_r - f_0$$

For the medical case, where the angle is the same on transmit and receive (transducer and target on same axis), and $v \ll c$ (non-relativistic, blood is much slower than sound), the final result simplifies to:

$$f_d = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$$

### Non-Relativistic Approximation (Verification)

Since $v \ll c$ (typical blood speed $\sim 0.5$ m/s vs $c = 1540$ m/s, ratio $\sim 3 \times 10^{-4}$), the term $(v/c)^2$ is negligible. Expanding the exact relativistic formula to first order in $v/c$ gives the medical Doppler equation.

**Quick numerical check:** $f_0 = 5$ MHz, $v = 0.5$ m/s, $\theta = 0°$:

$$f_d = \frac{2 \cdot (5 \times 10^6) \cdot 0.5 \cdot 1}{1540} \approx 3246 \text{ Hz} \approx 3.25 \text{ kHz}$$

This falls squarely in the audible range — which is why medical Doppler uses audio speakers to let clinicians *hear* the flow!

## 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Unambiguous Velocity (Nyquist Limit)

In Pulsed Wave (PW) Doppler, the system samples the Doppler signal at a rate equal to the **Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF)**. According to the **Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem**, to unambiguously reconstruct a signal, the sampling rate must be at least **twice** the highest frequency present in the signal.

$$f_{Nyquist} = \frac{\text{PRF}}{2}$$

Setting $f_d \le f_{Nyquist}$ and using $f_d = 2 f_0 v_{max} \cos\theta / c$ with $\theta = 0$ (worst case, max sensitivity):

$$\frac{2 f_0 v_{max}}{c} \le \frac{\text{PRF}}{2}$$

Solving for $v_{max}$:

$$v_{max} = \frac{c \cdot \text{PRF}}{4 f_0}$$

Since $\text{PRF} = 1/T_p$, this is equivalently:

$$v_{max} = \frac{c}{4 f_0 T_p}$$

### Numerical Example

For $f_0 = 5$ MHz, PRF = 10 kHz:

$$v_{max} = \frac{1540 \cdot 10000}{4 \cdot 5 \times 10^6} = 0.77 \text{ m/s}$$

If the actual blood velocity exceeds 0.77 m/s, **aliasing** occurs — the high-velocity flow appears as a flow in the *opposite direction* on the spectral display. Solutions to aliasing include: increasing PRF (deeper limit), decreasing $f_0$, or switching to **CW Doppler**.

## 3.3 Derivation of Depth-Range Relationship

The sample volume in PW Doppler is located at a depth:

$$d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

where $\Delta t$ is the time delay between the transmitted pulse and the received echo. The factor of 2 accounts for the round trip.

To avoid **range ambiguity** (where echoes from a *previous* pulse return after the next pulse is sent), we require:

$$d_{max} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot \text{PRF}}$$

This shows a fundamental **trade-off** in PW Doppler: **deeper imaging → lower PRF → lower maximum velocity (more aliasing)**.

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Quantity | Symbol | Increases with ↑ f₀ | Increases with ↑ PRF | Trade-off with depth |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Doppler shift $f_d$ | ↑ | ↑ | No direct effect | No |
| Max velocity $v_{max}$ | ↓ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ (deeper ⇒ lower PRF ⇒ lower $v_{max}$) |
| Resolution of velocity | ↓ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ |
| Penetration depth | ↑ | ↓ | ↓ | — |
| Aliasing risk | ↑ | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Doppler Ultrasound System — Block Architecture

The following Mermaid flowchart shows the signal flow of a typical Pulsed Wave Doppler ultrasound system, from transmission to spectral display.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Master Clock Oscillator] --> B[Pulse Generator]
    A --> C[Reference CW at f0]
    B --> D[Tx Transmitter Stage]
    C --> E[Mixer Down-conversion Stage]
    D --> F[Ultrasound Transducer Probe]
    F -->|Beam at angle theta| G[Moving Red Blood Cells]
    G -->|Back-scattered echo at f0 plus fd| F
    F --> H[Rx Preamplifier]
    H --> I[TGC Time Gain Compensation]
    I --> J[Quadrature Demodulator]
    E --> J
    J --> K[Range Gate Sample Volume Selector]
    K --> L[Wall Filter High Pass]
    L --> M[FFT Spectral Analyzer]
    M --> N[Spectral Display velocity vs time]
    M --> O[Audio Amplifier and Loudspeaker]
    M --> P[Autocorrelation Processor for Color Flow]
    P --> Q[Color Map Red toward Blue away]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The probe emits short pulses at frequency $f_0$ (typically 2–10 MHz).
- Echoes returning from blood cells are shifted by $\pm f_d$.
- The quadrature demodulator extracts $f_d$ by mixing the echo with the reference $f_0$.
- The range gate selects a specific depth for analysis.
- A high-pass wall filter removes low-frequency signals from moving vessel walls.
- The FFT produces the **spectral Doppler display** (velocity vs. time).

## 4.2 CW vs PW Doppler — Comparative Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CW[Continuous Wave Doppler]
        A1[Tx Crystal Oscillator] --> A2[Tx Probe Crystal]
        A3[Rx Probe Crystal] --> A4[Rx Amplifier]
        A2 -.Beam.- A5[Blood Flow]
        A5 -.Back-scatter.- A3
    end
    subgraph PW[Pulsed Wave Doppler]
        B1[Pulser Switch] --> B2[Single Crystal]
        B2 -->|Transmit pulse| B5[Blood Flow]
        B5 -->|Echo at fd| B2
        B2 --> B3[TR Switch Duplexer]
        B3 --> B4[Rx Range Gated]
    end
```

## 4.3 Color Doppler Encoding Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Acquire echo at sample volume] --> S[Compute phase shift phi between pulses]
    S --> T{phi greater than 0}
    T -->|Yes| U[Encode RED: flow toward transducer]
    T -->|No| V[Encode BLUE: flow away from transducer]
    U --> W[Saturation brightness proportional to mean velocity]
    V --> W
    W --> X[Variance map: green tint for turbulence]
```

## 4.4 Clinical Decision Tree — Aliasing Management

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Aliasing detected on spectral display] --> Q{Increase PRF possible}
    Q -->|Yes| R[Increase PRF: lowers vmax limit]
    Q -->|No| S[Lower transducer frequency f0]
    S --> T{Aliasing resolved}
    T -->|Yes| U[Continue PW Doppler]
    T -->|No| V[Switch to CW Doppler for high velocity]
    R --> W[Confirm by viewing spectral waveform]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these diagrams and not anatomical drawings:** KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes *system-level* understanding of imaging modalities. The block-level architecture flow demonstrates the **signal processing chain** that students are expected to reproduce in Module 1 viva voce examinations.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** Define Doppler shift and state the Doppler equation used in medical ultrasound. Mention the role of the angle of insonation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Doppler shift** ($f_d$) is the change in frequency of an ultrasound wave caused by relative motion between the source/observer and the reflecting target (e.g., red blood cells). The medical Doppler equation is:

$$f_d = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$$

where $f_0$ is the transmitted frequency, $v$ is the blood velocity, $\theta$ is the angle between the ultrasound beam and the direction of blood flow, and $c$ is the speed of sound in tissue (1540 m/s).

**Role of angle of insonation:** The $\cos\theta$ term indicates that Doppler sensitivity is maximum when the beam is **parallel** to flow ($\theta = 0°$) and **zero** when perpendicular ($\theta = 90°$). Clinical practice keeps $\theta < 60°$ to minimize velocity underestimation. **(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for equation, 1 for angle role.)**

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]** Differentiate between Continuous Wave (CW) and Pulsed Wave (PW) Doppler with respect to aliasing and depth selectivity.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | CW Doppler | PW Doppler |
|---|---|---|
| Aliasing | **No aliasing** (no sampling limit) | **Aliasing occurs** beyond $v_{max} = c \cdot \text{PRF}/(4f_0)$ |
| Depth selectivity | **None** — all velocities along the beam are detected simultaneously | **Yes** — operator selects a sample volume at a chosen depth using a range gate |
| Maximum velocity | **Unlimited** | Limited by Nyquist criterion |
| Typical use | High-velocity flows (cardiac valves) | General vessel flow studies |

**(3 marks: 1 aliasing, 1 depth, 1 use.)**

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the Doppler shift equation for an ultrasound system used in medical imaging. Clearly state the assumptions used. (7 marks)

**(b)** An ultrasound system operates at a transmitted frequency of 5 MHz. Blood flows in a vessel at a velocity of 0.6 m/s. The ultrasound beam makes an angle of 30° with the direction of flow. Calculate the Doppler shift. (Assume speed of sound in tissue $c = 1540$ m/s.) (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

1. **Assumption 1:** The transducer is stationary; only the blood cells (reflectors) move. (1 mark)
2. **Assumption 2:** Velocity of blood is much less than the speed of sound in tissue ($v \ll c$), so non-relativistic Doppler applies. (1 mark)
3. **Assumption 3:** The angle $\theta$ between the beam and the flow direction is the same on transmit and receive (since the back-scattered wave returns along the same path). (1 mark)

**Stage 1 (transmit):** Frequency perceived by the moving blood cell:

$$f' = f_0 \left( \frac{c - v\cos\theta}{c} \right)$$

**Stage 2 (receive):** Frequency received by the stationary transducer from the moving cell:

$$f_r = f' \left( \frac{c}{c - v\cos\theta} \right)$$

Substituting:

$$f_r = f_0 \left( \frac{c - v\cos\theta}{c} \right) \cdot \left( \frac{c}{c - v\cos\theta} \right) = f_0$$

This is the **stationary** case. For the Doppler shift between the **two-way** paths, careful sign analysis (the cell moves **toward** the transducer on the return path as well when flow is toward Tx) gives:

$$f_d = f_r - f_0 = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$$

(4 marks for full derivation)

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

Given:
- $f_0 = 5 \text{ MHz} = 5 \times 10^6$ Hz
- $v = 0.6$ m/s
- $\theta = 30°$, so $\cos 30° = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866$
- $c = 1540$ m/s

Applying the Doppler equation:

$$f_d = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$$

$$f_d = \frac{2 \cdot (5 \times 10^6) \cdot 0.6 \cdot 0.866}{1540}$$

$$f_d = \frac{5.196 \times 10^6}{1540}$$

$$f_d \approx 3374 \text{ Hz} \approx 3.37 \text{ kHz}$$

**Answer:** The Doppler shift is approximately **3.37 kHz**, which lies in the audible range. (7 marks: 1 for setup, 2 for substitution, 2 for arithmetic, 2 for unit/final answer.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the principle of Color Doppler imaging. Describe the role of the autocorrelation processor and the color map. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF) of 8 kHz and a transmitted frequency of 4 MHz, calculate the maximum unambiguous velocity for PW Doppler. If a patient's blood velocity in the carotid artery is measured as 1.2 m/s, will aliasing occur? What is the remedy? (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Color Doppler (7 marks):**

**Principle (3 marks):** Color Doppler imaging uses **pulsed wave** ultrasound to estimate blood flow velocity at *multiple* sample volumes along each scan line (typically 4–16). It encodes the **mean phase shift** between successive echoes into a color overlay on a B-mode grayscale anatomical image.

**Autocorrelation (2 marks):** The **autocorrelation processor** computes the average phase shift $\Delta\phi$ between consecutive received pulses for each sample volume:

$$v = \frac{c \cdot \Delta\phi}{4 \pi f_0 T_p}$$

where $T_p$ is the pulse repetition period. This computation is performed *in real time* for thousands of sample volumes, producing a color-coded velocity map.

**Color map (2 marks):**
- **Red** = flow **toward** transducer (positive $\Delta\phi$)
- **Blue** = flow **away from** transducer (negative $\Delta\phi$)
- **Brightness/saturation** = magnitude of velocity
- **Green tint** = variance (turbulence)

**Part (b) — Maximum Velocity and Aliasing (7 marks):**

Given:
- PRF = 8 kHz = 8000 Hz
- $f_0 = 4$ MHz = $4 \times 10^6$ Hz
- $c = 1540$ m/s
- Measured velocity $v = 1.2$ m/s

**Step 1: Calculate $v_{max}$ using the Nyquist limit formula:**

$$v_{max} = \frac{c \cdot \text{PRF}}{4 f_0}$$

$$v_{max} = \frac{1540 \cdot 8000}{4 \cdot 4 \times 10^6}$$

$$v_{max} = \frac{12.32 \times 10^6}{16 \times 10^6}$$

$$v_{max} = 0.77 \text{ m/s}$$

(3 marks for calculation)

**Step 2: Compare with measured velocity:**

$$v_{measured} = 1.2 \text{ m/s} > v_{max} = 0.77 \text{ m/s}$$

Since the measured velocity exceeds the maximum unambiguous velocity, **aliasing will occur**. The high-velocity flow will appear wrapped around and displayed as flow in the opposite direction on the spectral display. (2 marks for diagnosis)

**Step 3: Remedies (2 marks):**

1. **Increase the PRF** (e.g., to 16 kHz) to raise $v_{max}$ to 1.54 m/s, eliminating aliasing. (Limited by imaging depth — pulses must return before next is sent.)
2. **Decrease the transmitted frequency** $f_0$ (e.g., from 4 MHz to 2 MHz), which doubles $v_{max}$ to 1.54 m/s.
3. **Switch to CW Doppler** which has no aliasing limit (loses depth resolution).
4. **Shift the baseline** of the spectral display to extend the scale in one direction.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget the **factor of 4** in the denominator (from $2 \cdot 2$ — the 2 from two-way Doppler and the 2 from Nyquist sampling). Writing $v_{max} = c \cdot \text{PRF}/(2f_0)$ is a common 1-mark deduction error. Also, never state the units of $f_d$ as "Hz" and $v$ as "m/s" interchangeably; maintain dimensional consistency throughout.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] The **Doppler equation**: $f_d = 2 f_0 v \cos\theta / c$. Memorize the four variables and the constant $c = 1540$ m/s.
- [ ] The factor **"2"** in the numerator accounts for the **two-way** (round-trip) Doppler shift.
- [ ] The **cos θ** term: zero Doppler at $\theta = 90°$; maximum at $\theta = 0°$; clinical optimum $\theta < 60°$.
- [ ] **CW Doppler**: two crystals, no aliasing, no depth resolution. Used for **high velocities** (e.g., aortic stenosis).
- [ ] **PW Doppler**: one crystal, depth-selective, aliasing limited. **Nyquist limit**: $v_{max} = c \cdot \text{PRF}/(4 f_0)$.
- [ ] **Aliasing** appears as "wrap-around" of the spectral waveform. Remedies: ↑ PRF, ↓ $f_0$, switch to CW.
- [ ] **Color Doppler**: red = toward, blue = away, brightness = magnitude, green = variance. Uses autocorrelation.
- [ ] **Power Doppler**: encodes *energy* of Doppler signal, angle-independent, no aliasing — used for low flow.
- [ ] **Wall filter** (high-pass filter) removes low-frequency signals from vessel wall motion, typically 50–200 Hz cutoff.
- [ ] **Sample volume**: the localized region of interest in PW Doppler, depth = $c \cdot \Delta t / 2$.
- [ ] Doppler shift falls in the **audible range (100 Hz – 10 kHz)** — clinicians literally *listen* to blood flow.
- [ ] Typical transmitted frequencies: **2 MHz** (deep cardiac), **5 MHz** (general abdominal), **7–10 MHz** (superficial vascular).
- [ ] Range–velocity trade-off: deeper scanning → lower PRF → lower $v_{max}$ → more aliasing.
- [ ] Reynolds number > 2000 indicates **turbulent flow**; Doppler shows spectral broadening and green color variance.
- [ ] Reynolds formula: $\text{Re} = \rho v D / \mu$, with $\rho \approx 1060$ kg/m³, $\mu \approx 3 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Magnetic resonance imaging: Principles of MRI
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT721 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Magnetic resonance imaging: Principles of MRI

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)** is a non-invasive, non-ionizing medical imaging modality that exploits the quantum mechanical property of **nuclear spin** in hydrogen protons (¹H) to generate high-resolution, multi-planar cross-sectional images of the human body. The technique is governed by the principle of **Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR)**, wherein protons aligned within a strong static external magnetic field **B₀** absorb and re-emit radiofrequency (RF) energy. The spatial distribution of this re-emitted signal is encoded using **magnetic field gradients**, and the image is reconstructed mathematically using the **Fourier Transform**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, MRI is positioned as a hybrid of *applied physics* (quantum mechanics, electromagnetism) and *digital signal processing* (k-space, FFT reconstruction).

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy: "The Spinning Compass Inside a Megaphone"

Imagine billions of tiny invisible **spinning tops** (protons) scattered randomly inside your body. Normally, they spin in all directions, cancelling each other out — like a chaotic crowd shouting.

When you switch on the MRI scanner, a **massive magnet (B₀)** lines them all up, like a drill sergeant commanding every soldier to face North. They still spin, but they **wobble** in unison around the magnetic field direction — this wobble is called **precession**.

Now, the scanner blasts a brief **radio pulse** (like a megaphone shouting "TAG!") at a precise frequency. Only those tops wobbling at exactly that frequency absorb the shout and tip sideways into a synchronised, detectable wave.

When the megaphone stops, the tops relax back to their original positions, each at a slightly different rate depending on the tissue they're in (fat relaxes fast, water relaxes slow). The scanner listens to this fading chorus with sensitive antennas, decodes the "voices" by their pitch (frequency) and timing (phase), and the computer paints a picture — tissue by tissue.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Hydrogen?** The human body is ~63% water by mass, and water (H₂O) is rich in hydrogen nuclei (¹H). Hydrogen has the highest **gyromagnetic ratio (γ = 42.58 MHz/T)** among biologically abundant nuclei, making it the most NMR-sensitive. This is the foundational reason MRI is so effective for soft-tissue imaging.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants (Must Memorise for KTU)

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|-------|------|
| Gyromagnetic ratio of ¹H | γ | **42.58** | MHz/T |
| Planck's constant | h | **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴** | J·s |
| Bohr magneton | μ_B | **9.274 × 10⁻²⁴** | J/T |
| Typical clinical B₀ | B₀ | **1.5 – 3.0** | Tesla (T) |
| Larmor frequency at 1.5 T | f₀ | **63.87** | MHz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The term *"Principles of MRI"* in Module 1 expects students to master the chain: *Spin → Precession → Resonance → Relaxation → Signal → Encoding → Reconstruction*. Each link is a high-yield 3-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Physics of MRI Signal Generation

The MRI pipeline is a five-stage causal chain. Each stage is examinable in isolation.

#### Stage 1 — Proton Spin & the Net Magnetisation Vector (M₀)

- Hydrogen's nucleus contains a single proton, which possesses an intrinsic **angular momentum** (spin quantum number I = ½).
- This spin produces a tiny **magnetic moment** μ.
- In the absence of an external field, individual proton moments point in random directions, so the vector sum is **M = 0**.
- When **B₀** is applied along the z-axis, the moments partially align *parallel* (low energy, N↑ state) or *anti-parallel* (high energy, N↓ state).
- Per **Boltzmann statistics**, the *excess* of parallel spins produces a small but measurable **net longitudinal magnetisation M₀** along +z.

$$M_0 = \frac{N \gamma^2 \hbar^2 B_0}{4 k_B T}$$

where N is the number of spins per unit volume, k_B is Boltzmann's constant, and T is absolute temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> At 1.5 T, only ~5 protons per million are in the excess state — yet this tiny imbalance is enough to generate the entire MRI signal.

#### Stage 2 — Larmor Precession

Once aligned, the magnetisation vector **M** does not point statically along B₀. Instead, it **precesses** (rotates in a cone) around the z-axis at the **Larmor frequency**.

$$\omega_0 = \gamma B_0 \quad \text{or} \quad f_0 = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$$

**Why does it precess?** The magnetic moment experiences a torque τ = μ × B, which continuously reorients it without aligning it — analogous to a spinning gyroscope under gravity.

#### Stage 3 — RF Excitation & Resonance

- The scanner applies a brief (typically 1–10 ms) RF pulse at frequency **f₀** via a transmitter coil, generating an oscillating magnetic field **B₁** in the transverse (x–y) plane.
- At exact resonance, the protons absorb energy and the net magnetisation vector **tips** away from the z-axis by a **flip angle α**.
- Common flip angles: **90°** (saturation recovery, spin echo) and **180°** (inversion, refocusing).

$$\alpha = \gamma \int_0^{T_p} B_1(t) \, dt$$

After a 90° pulse, **M₀** now lies entirely in the transverse plane — the maximum possible measurable signal.

#### Stage 4 — Free Induction Decay (FID) and Relaxation

When the RF pulse is switched off, two independent relaxation processes begin simultaneously:

- **T1 Relaxation (Longitudinal / Spin-Lattice):** The excited protons release energy to the surrounding molecular lattice (the "environment"). M_z grows back to M₀.

$$M_z(t) = M_0 \left(1 - e^{-t/T_1}\right)$$

- **T2 Relaxation (Transverse / Spin-Spin):** Individual proton spins lose phase coherence due to spin-spin interactions. M_xy decays to zero.

$$M_{xy}(t) = M_{xy}(0) \, e^{-t/T_2}$$

- The measured decay (called **Free Induction Decay, FID**) is the actual MRI signal picked up by the receiver coil.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **T1 vs T2 — Examiner's Favourite Distinction:**
> - T1 = energy exchange with surroundings (sensitive to molecular motion).
> - T2 = phase coherence loss (sensitive to local field inhomogeneities).
> - Clinically: **Fat has SHORT T1** (bright on T1-weighted images), **Water/CSF has LONG T2** (bright on T2-weighted images).

#### Stage 5 — Spatial Encoding (Slice, Phase, Frequency)

A uniform B₀ alone cannot produce an image — every proton would emit the same frequency. The scanner uses three orthogonal **gradient coils** to make the magnetic field (and hence the Larmor frequency) a linear function of position:

- **Slice-Selection Gradient (G_ss)** along z: Only protons in a thin slice resonate with the RF pulse frequency.
- **Phase-Encoding Gradient (G_pe)** along y: Briefly applied to give each row of voxels a unique phase.
- **Frequency-Encoding / Readout Gradient (G_fe)** along x: Spreads frequencies linearly across columns during signal acquisition.

The relationship between position **r = (x, y, z)** and the local field becomes:

$$B(\mathbf{r}) = B_0 + G_x x + G_y y + G_z z$$

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | KTU Use |
|---|---------|------------------|---------|
| 1 | $f_0 = \gamma B_0$ | Larmor frequency | Resonance condition |
| 2 | $M_z(t) = M_0 (1 - e^{-t/T_1})$ | T1 regrowth | T1-weighting derivation |
| 3 | $M_{xy}(t) = M_{xy}(0) e^{-t/T_2}$ | T2 decay | T2-weighting derivation |
| 4 | $\alpha = \gamma B_1 T_p$ | Flip angle | Ernst angle calc |
| 5 | $\cos\alpha = e^{-TR/T_1}$ | Ernst angle formula | Sequence optimisation |
| 6 | $S(k) = \int \rho(\mathbf{r}) e^{-i 2\pi \mathbf{k} \cdot \mathbf{r}} d\mathbf{r}$ | Fourier encoding | Image reconstruction |
| 7 | $\rho(\mathbf{r}) = \int S(\mathbf{k}) e^{+i 2\pi \mathbf{k} \cdot \mathbf{r}} d\mathbf{k}$ | Inverse FT | k-space to image |
| 8 | $\Delta \omega = \gamma G_x \Delta x$ | Frequency-position map | Bandwidth & FOV |
| 9 | $FOV = \frac{BW}{\gamma G_{read}}$ | Field of view | Scan parameter design |
| 10 | $\text{SNR} \propto B_0^{1.5 \text{ to } 2}$ | Signal-to-noise | Field strength comparison |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder for Markdown Tables:** In all equations above, the absolute value or norm is written using `\vert` (e.g., $\vert M_{xy} \vert$) to avoid breaking the table's `|` column delimiter. This is a strict formatting rule under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Diagnostic Radiology:** Brain, spine, joint, and cardiac imaging (T1, T2, FLAIR, DWI).
- **Functional MRI (fMRI):** Detects Blood Oxygen Level Dependent (BOLD) signal changes.
- **Spectroscopic MRI:** Maps metabolite concentrations (NAA, Choline, Lactate).
- **Interventional MRI:** Real-time guidance of surgical instruments and ablation probes.
- **Materials Science:** NMR microscopy for porous media and polymers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Larmor Precession from the Bloch Equation

**Starting Point:** The classical equation of motion for a magnetic moment μ in a magnetic field B is the **torque equation**:

$$\frac{d\mathbf{M}}{dt} = \gamma \mathbf{M} \times \mathbf{B}$$

**Step 1 — Apply the static field.** With B = (0, 0, B₀), expand the cross product:

$$\frac{dM_x}{dt} = \gamma (M_y B_0 - M_z \cdot 0) = \gamma B_0 M_y$$

$$\frac{dM_y}{dt} = \gamma (M_z \cdot 0 - M_x B_0) = -\gamma B_0 M_x$$

$$\frac{dM_z}{dt} = \gamma (M_x \cdot 0 - M_y \cdot 0) = 0$$

**Step 2 — Interpret M_z.** The third equation shows M_z is constant in time — the *longitudinal* component is conserved (relaxation is added later).

**Step 3 — Solve the coupled transverse equations.** Differentiate the M_x equation and substitute M_y:

$$\frac{d^2 M_x}{dt^2} = \gamma B_0 \frac{dM_y}{dt} = \gamma B_0 (-\gamma B_0 M_x) = -\gamma^2 B_0^2 M_x$$

**Step 4 — Identify the angular frequency.** This is the SHM equation $d^2 M_x / dt^2 = -\omega_0^2 M_x$, giving:

$$\omega_0 = \gamma B_0 \quad \text{(Larmor Frequency)}$$

**Step 5 — General solution.** The transverse components precess in the x-y plane:

$$M_x(t) = M_\perp \cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$$
$$M_y(t) = -M_\perp \sin(\omega_0 t + \phi)$$

where $M_\perp = \sqrt{M_x^2 + M_y^2}$ is the constant transverse magnitude and φ is the initial phase. **Physical interpretation:** The magnetisation vector M traces a circle of radius M_⊥ around the z-axis at angular frequency ω₀.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: T1 and T2 Relaxation Solutions

**Starting Point:** Add phenomenological relaxation terms to the Bloch equation (valid for an ensemble of spins in a uniform field):

$$\frac{dM_x}{dt} = \gamma (\mathbf{M} \times \mathbf{B})_x - \frac{M_x}{T_2}$$

$$\frac{dM_y}{dt} = \gamma (\mathbf{M} \times \mathbf{B})_y - \frac{M_y}{T_2}$$

$$\frac{dM_z}{dt} = \gamma (\mathbf{M} \times \mathbf{B})_z + \frac{M_0 - M_z}{T_1}$$

**Step 1 — Longitudinal (T1) equation.** With B = (0, 0, B₀), the cross product's z-component is zero, so:

$$\frac{dM_z}{dt} = \frac{M_0 - M_z}{T_1}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange into separable form.**

$$T_1 \frac{dM_z}{M_0 - M_z} = dt$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from t = 0 (where M_z = 0 after a 90° pulse) to t.**

$$-T_1 \ln(M_0 - M_z) \Big|_{0}^{t} = t$$

$$-T_1 \ln\!\left(\frac{M_0 - M_z(t)}{M_0}\right) = t$$

**Step 4 — Solve for M_z(t).** Exponentiating both sides:

$$\frac{M_0 - M_z(t)}{M_0} = e^{-t/T_1}$$

$$\boxed{M_z(t) = M_0 \left(1 - e^{-t/T_1}\right)}$$

**Step 5 — Transverse (T2) equation.** With B along z, $\frac{dM_x}{dt} = \gamma B_0 M_y - \frac{M_x}{T_2}$ and similarly for M_y. In the rotating frame at ω₀, the precession terms vanish, leaving only decay:

$$\frac{dM_\perp}{dt} = -\frac{M_\perp}{T_2}$$

**Step 6 — Solve.** Direct integration gives:

$$\boxed{M_{xy}(t) = M_{xy}(0) \, e^{-t/T_2}}$$

**Step 7 — Physical meaning of T1 and T2.**
- At $t = T_1$: $M_z = M_0(1 - e^{-1}) \approx 0.632 M_0$ → **63.2%** of longitudinal magnetisation recovered.
- At $t = T_2$: $M_{xy} = M_{xy}(0) e^{-1} \approx 0.368 M_{xy}(0)$ → **36.8%** of transverse magnetisation remains.

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3: 2-D Fourier Transform Image Reconstruction

**Step 1 — The received signal S(t) is a sum of contributions from all protons:**

$$S(t) = \int \int \int \rho(x,y,z) \, e^{-i 2\pi [k_x(t) x + k_y(t) y + k_z(t) z]} \, dx \, dy \, dz$$

where ρ(x,y,z) is the proton spin density and k_x, k_y, k_z are the cumulative gradient moments:

$$k_x(t) = \frac{\gamma}{2\pi} \int_0^t G_x(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 2 — Restrict to a 2-D slice (z = constant) for clarity:**

$$S(k_x, k_y) = \iint \rho(x,y) \, e^{-i 2\pi (k_x x + k_y y)} \, dx \, dy$$

**Step 3 — Recognise the form.** This is exactly the **2-D Fourier Transform** of ρ(x,y). Therefore ρ is the inverse 2-D Fourier Transform of S:

$$\rho(x,y) = \iint S(k_x, k_y) \, e^{+i 2\pi (k_x x + k_y y)} \, dk_x \, dk_y$$

**Step 4 — Discretise for the digital scanner.** Replace integrals with sums; the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) computes:

$$\rho[m,n] = \frac{1}{MN} \sum_{p=0}^{M-1} \sum_{q=0}^{N-1} S[p,q] \, e^{+i 2\pi (pm/M + qn/N)}$$

**Step 5 — K-space interpretation.** The raw data S(k_x, k_y) is called **k-space**. Each acquired FID (one line) fills one row of k-space. The image is a 2-D FFT of the populated k-space matrix.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the conjugate sign flip?** A physics convention uses $e^{-i\omega t}$ for forward time evolution, while an engineering convention uses $e^{-j\omega t}$ for the forward Fourier transform. The image-domain inverse therefore carries the opposite sign — a frequent KTU exam pitfall.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Sequence Parameters vs Contrast

| Sequence | TR | TE | Weighting | Fat Appearance | Water Appearance | Best For |
|----------|-----|-----|-----------|----------------|------------------|----------|
| Spin Echo T1 | **Short** (<800 ms) | **Short** (<30 ms) | T1-weighted | **Bright** | Dark | Anatomy |
| Spin Echo T2 | **Long** (>2000 ms) | **Long** (>80 ms) | T2-weighted | Dark | **Bright** | Pathology/Edema |
| FLAIR | Long | Long | T2 (CSF nulled) | Dark | Dark (CSF), Bright (lesion) | MS lesions |
| STIR | Long | Short | T1 (fat nulled) | **Dark** | Bright | Fat suppression |
| Gradient Echo | Variable | Variable | T2* | Variable | Variable | Speed/blood |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiners' trap:** A "T2-weighted" image with TR = 500 ms is a contradiction in disguise — T2-weighting requires TR ≫ T1. Always verify both TR AND TE before naming the contrast.

---

### 3.5 Exhaustive Numerical Example: Larmor Frequency at 3 T

**Problem:** A proton in a 3 T MRI scanner is at position x = 5 cm off-isocentre while a frequency-encoding gradient G_x = 4 mT/m is active. Calculate (a) the Larmor frequency at isocentre, (b) the Larmor frequency at the off-centre location, and (c) the bandwidth per pixel for a 256 × 256 matrix over a 24 cm FOV.

**Solution:**

**(a)** At isocentre, B = B₀ = 3 T:

$$f_0 = \gamma B_0 = 42.58 \text{ MHz/T} \times 3 \text{ T} = 127.74 \text{ MHz}$$

**(b)** At x = 5 cm, the total field is:

$$B = B_0 + G_x \cdot x = 3 + (4 \times 10^{-3})(0.05) = 3.0002 \text{ T}$$

$$f = 42.58 \times 3.0002 = 127.7485 \text{ MHz}$$

So the off-centre proton resonates **85 Hz higher** than the isocentre proton — this small difference is precisely what the receiver decodes into spatial position.

**(c)** Bandwidth per pixel:

$$\Delta f_{\text{pixel}} = \frac{\gamma \cdot G_x \cdot FOV}{N_{read}} = \frac{42.58 \times 10^6 \times 4 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.24}{256} = 159.7 \text{ Hz/pixel}$$

This is a typical receiver bandwidth value used in clinical protocols.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 MRI System Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Host Computer - Pulse Sequence] --> B[Pulse Sequence Generator]
    B --> C[Gradient Amplifier]
    B --> D[RF Transmitter]
    B --> E[Timing and Control Unit]
    C --> F[Gradient Coils X Y Z]
    D --> G[RF Transmit Coil]
    F --> H[Patient Bore B0 1.5T to 3T]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Receiver RF Coil]
    I --> J[Low Noise Preamplifier]
    J --> K[Analog to Digital Converter]
    K --> L[Digital Signal Processor]
    L --> M[K Space Raw Data Buffer]
    M --> N[2D Inverse FFT]
    N --> O[Image Domain Magnitude Map]
    O --> P[Display and DICOM Archive]
```

### 4.2 MRI Signal Generation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Hydrogen Protons in Body] --> P2[Apply Static Field B0]
    P2 --> P3[Net Magnetisation M0 along Z]
    P3 --> P4[RF Pulse at Larmor Frequency]
    P4 --> P5[Tip M0 into XY Plane]
    P5 --> P6[Apply Gradients for Spatial Encoding]
    P6 --> P7[Free Induction Decay Signal]
    P7 --> P8[Receive and Digitise]
    P8 --> P9[Fill K Space]
    P9 --> P10[Inverse 2D FFT]
    P10 --> P11[Reconstructed MR Image]
```

```

```
