# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**
**Q: Distinguish between a band-pass filter and a band-stop filter in the frequency domain. Mention one medical imaging application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **band-pass filter (BPF)** allows only a specific annular region of frequencies (between $D_{\text{low}}$ and $D_{\text{high}}$) to pass, attenuating all frequencies outside this ring. In contrast, a **band-stop filter (BSF)** does the opposite — it attenuates the annular region and passes all frequencies outside it.

Mathematically, for the ideal case:

$$
H_{BPF}(u,v) + H_{BSF}(u,v) = 1 \quad \forall (u,v)
$$

**Medical applications:**
- **BPF:** Isolating texture patterns of lung tissue in chest X-rays or extracting mid-frequency speckle patterns in ultrasound for tumor characterization.
- **BSF:** Removing periodic ring artifacts in CT reconstruction or eliminating 50/60 Hz power-line interference in MRI scans.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
**Q: How is a band-pass filter constructed from a low-pass and a high-pass filter? Show with the transfer function.**

**Model Answer:**

A band-pass filter can be constructed as the **pointwise product** of a low-pass filter of radius $D_{\text{high}}$ and a high-pass filter of radius $D_{\text{low}}$:

$$
\boxed{H_{BPF}(u,v) = H_{LP}(u,v; D_{\text{high}}) \cdot H_{HP}(u,v; D_{\text{low}})}
$$

The LPF passes all frequencies $\le D_{\text{high}}$, and the HPF passes all frequencies $> D_{\text{low}}$. Their intersection is the **annular band** $[D_{\text{low}}, D_{\text{high}}]$ — exactly the pass region of a band-pass filter. This is also equivalent to:

$$
H_{BPF}(u,v) = H_{LP}(u,v; D_{\text{high}}) - H_{LP}(u,v; D_{\text{low}})
$$

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the shift:** Students frequently forget that the DFT must be **center-shifted** (using $(-1)^{x+y}$ multiplication) so that the zero-frequency (DC) component is at the center of the frequency plane. Without this, your annulus is drawn in the wrong corner. **(−2 marks)**
> 2. **Confusing $D_0$ with $W$:** $D_0$ is the **center (radius) of the annulus**, NOT the bandwidth. $W$ is the bandwidth. **(−1 to −2 marks)**
> 3. **Ideal filter ringing:** Students write "Ideal filter is best" — WRONG. Always mention the **Gibbs phenomenon / ringing artifacts** for the ideal filter. **(−1 mark)**
> 4. **Forgetting the inverse DFT and taking real part:** The output of the inverse DFT is generally complex due to numerical errors; you must take the **real part** to get the filtered image. **(−1 mark)**
> 5. **Not stating the complementarity relation** when comparing BPF and BSF: examiners explicitly look for $H_{BPF} + H_{BSF} = 1$. **(−1 mark)**

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]`**

**(a)** Define an **ideal band-pass filter** and an **ideal band-stop filter**. Derive the transfer function of an ideal band-pass filter as the product of a low-pass and a high-pass filter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a **Butterworth band-pass filter of order $n$**, derive the transfer function $H_{BPF,n}(u,v)$ and explain how the choice of $n$ affects the filtered medical image. Mention one situation in medical imaging where this filter is preferred over the ideal filter. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to B-A(a):

**Step 1 — Definitions:**

An **ideal band-pass filter (IBPF)** passes without attenuation all frequency components whose distance $D(u,v)$ from the origin lies in the closed interval $[D_0 - W/2,\ D_0 + W/2]$, where $D_0$ is the central radius and $W$ is the bandwidth of the band.

$$
H_{IBPF}(u,v) =
\begin{cases}
1, & D_0 - \dfrac{W}{2} \le D(u,v) \le D_0 + \dfrac{W}{2} \\
0, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

An **ideal band-stop filter (IBSF)** is its complement:

$$
H_{IBSF}(u,v) =
\begin{cases}
0, & D_0 - \dfrac{W}{2} \le D(u,v) \le D_0 + \dfrac{W}{2} \\
1, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 2 — Derivation by series product:**

Take an ideal low-pass filter of cutoff radius $D_{\text{high}} = D_0 + W/2$:

$$
H_{LP}(u,v) = \begin{cases} 1, & D(u,v) \le D_{\text{high}} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

And an ideal high-pass filter of cutoff radius $D_{\text{low}} = D_0 - W/2$:

$$
H_{HP}(u,v) = \begin{cases} 0, & D(u,v) \le D_{\text{low}} \\ 1, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

Multiplying pointwise:

$$
H_{LP}(u,v) \cdot H_{HP}(u,v) =
\begin{cases}
1 \cdot 0 = 0, & D(u,v) \le D_{\text{low}} \\
1 \cdot 1 = 1, & D_{\text{low}} < D(u,v) \le D_{\text{high}} \\
0 \cdot 1 = 0, & D(u,v) > D_{\text{high}}
\end{cases}
$$

This is non-zero only in the annulus $[D_{\text{low}},\ D_{\text{high}}]$, which is precisely $H_{IBPF}$. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{H_{IBPF}(u,v) = H_{LP}(u,v; D_0 + W/2) \cdot H_{HP}(u,v; D_0 - W/2)}
$$

#### Model Solution to B-A(b):

**Step 1 — Start from Butterworth LPF of order $n$:**

$$
H_{LP,n}(u,v; D_c) = \frac{1}{1 + \left[\dfrac{D(u,v)}{D_c}\right]^{2n}}
$$

**Step 2 — Derive Butterworth HPF as the complement:**

$$
H_{HP,n}(u,v; D_c) = 1 - H_{LP,n}(u,v; D_c) = \frac{1}{1 + \left[\dfrac{D_c}{D(u,v)}\right]^{2n}}
$$

**Step 3 — Form the BPF as a product, with $D_c = D_{\text{high}}$ for LPF and $D_c = D_{\text{low}}$ for HPF, and simplify (see Section 3.3):**

$$
H_{BPF,n}(u,v) = \frac{1}{1 + \left[\dfrac{D(u,v) \cdot W}{D^2(u,v) - D_0^2}\right]^{2n}}
$$

**Step 4 — Effect of order $n$:**

- **$n = 1$:** Very smooth, gentle roll-off; minimal ringing; suitable for diagnostic medical images.
- **$n = 2$:** Moderate sharpness; small ringing; commonly used in clinical pipelines.
- **Higher $n$ ($3, 4, \ldots$):** Approaches the ideal brick-wall behavior but introduces **pronounced ringing artifacts** in the spatial domain due to oscillations in the transfer function's impulse response (sinc-like sidelobes).

**Step 5 — Medical imaging situation where Butterworth BPF is preferred over Ideal BPF:**

The Butterworth BPF is preferred in **CT image texture analysis** (e.g., characterizing lung nodule patterns). The ideal BPF produces strong **Gibbs ringing** around sharp structures (e.g., near bone-tissue interfaces), which mimics or obscures real pathology. The smooth roll-off of the Butterworth filter at low order ($n=2$) suppresses this artifact while still providing adequate frequency selectivity.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]`**

**(a)** What is a **band-stop filter**? Explain the **ideal, Butterworth, and Gaussian** band-stop filters with their transfer functions. State the **complementarity relation** with band-pass filters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A chest X-ray image $f(x,y)$ of size $512 \times 512$ is suspected to contain **periodic ring artifacts** centered at the DC component. Design a **Gaussian band-stop filter** with $D_0 = 30$ and bandwidth $W = 10$ cycles/image to suppress these artifacts. Write the transfer function, describe the filtering procedure, and explain why a Gaussian (not Ideal) BSF is preferred for diagnostic imaging. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to B-B(a):

**Step 1 — Definition:**

A **band-stop (band-reject) filter (BSF)** is a frequency-domain filter that **attenuates (rejects) frequencies inside a defined annular band** $D_0 - W/2 \le D(u,v) \le D_0 + W/2$ and **passes all other frequencies** unchanged. It is the **complement** of the band-pass filter.

**Step 2 — Ideal BSF:**

$$
H_{IBSF}(u,v) = \begin{cases} 0, & D_0 - W/2 \le D \le D_0 + W/2 \\ 1, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 3 — Butterworth BSF (order $n$):**

$$
H_{BBSF,n}(u,v) = \frac{1}{1 + \left[\dfrac{D(u,v) \cdot W}{D^2(u,v) - D_0^2}\right]^{-2n}}
$$

This is **1 at the center** ($D = D_0$, since the negative exponent makes the term 0) and **falls to 0** at the band edges.

**Step 4 — Gaussian BSF:**

$$
H_{GBSF}(u,v) = \exp\!\left[-\frac{1}{2}\!\left(\frac{D^2(u,v) - D_0^2}{D(u,v) \cdot W}\right)^{\!2}\right]
$$

The Gaussian profile is **smooth, monotonic, and non-oscillatory** — never producing negative values or ringing.

**Step 5 — Complementarity relation:**

For all three variants, pointwise:

$$
\boxed{H_{BSF}(u,v) + H_{BPF}(u,v) = 1 \quad \forall (u,v)}
$$

Hence $H_{BSF}(u,v) = 1 - H_{BPF}(u,v)$ is the standard construction identity.

#### Model Solution to B-B(b):

**Step 1 — Required parameters:**

- Image size: $M = N = 512$
- Center frequency: $D_0 = 30$ cycles/image
- Bandwidth: $W = 10$ cycles/image
- Inner radius: $D_{\text{low}} = D_0 - W/2 = 25$
- Outer radius: $D_{\text{high}} = D_0 + W/2 = 35$

**Step 2 — Transfer function:**

For each frequency coordinate $(u,v)$ with $u, v \in [-256, 255]$:

$$
D(u,v) = \sqrt{u^2 + v^2}
$$

$$
H_{GBSF}(u,v) = \exp\!\left[-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{D^2(u,v) - 30^2}{D(u,v) \cdot 10}\right)^{\!2}\right] = \exp\!\left[-\frac{(D^2 - 900)^2}{200 \cdot D^2}\right]
$$

with the convention that $H(0,0) = 1$ (DC is preserved since ring artifacts don't include DC).

**Step 3 — Filtering procedure:**

1. Compute the **2D DFT** $F(u,v) = \mathcal{F}\{f(x,y)\}$ of the input $512 \times 512$ image.
2. **Center-shift** by multiplying $f(x,y)$ with $(-1)^{x+y}$ before the DFT (so the origin is at the center of the frequency plane).
3. Construct $H_{GBSF}(u,v)$ on a $512 \times 512$ grid using the formula above.
4. Form the **filtered spectrum**: $G(u,v) = H_{GBSF}(u,v) \cdot F(u,v)$.
5. Apply **inverse DFT**: $g_p(x,y) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{G(u,v)\}$.
6. **Un-shift** by multiplying with $(-1)^{x+y}$.
7. Take the **real part** of $g_p(x,y)$ to get the final filtered image: $g(x,y) = \text{Re}\{g_p(x,y)\}$.

**Step 4 — Why Gaussian is preferred over Ideal BSF for diagnostic imaging:**

- The **ideal BSF** has a **sharp, discontinuous** transfer function. Its inverse DFT (the spatial-domain filter kernel) is a **sinc-like function with strong oscillating sidelobes**. When convolved with the image, these sidelobes produce **ringing artifacts** (Gibbs phenomenon) — visible as **false halos and ripples around sharp anatomical structures** like ribs, lung edges, and tumor boundaries. In diagnostic X-rays, such halos can **mimic pathology** or **obscure subtle lesions**, leading to misdiagnosis.
- The **Gaussian BSF** has a **smooth, infinitely differentiable** transfer function with **no oscillations**. Its inverse DFT is also Gaussian (and real, positive, non-oscillatory). The resulting filtered image is **artifact-free**, with only the targeted frequency band smoothly attenuated.
- The Gaussian is therefore the **clinically safe** choice for diagnostic preprocessing.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ A **band-pass filter (BPF)** passes an **annular ring** of frequencies; a **band-stop filter (BSF)** rejects it.
- ✅ Both are **complementary**: $H_{BPF} + H_{BSF} = 1$ pointwise.
- ✅ BPF construction: $H_{BPF} = H_{LP}(D_{\text{high}}) \cdot H_{HP}(D_{\text{low}})$ (series product).
- ✅ Alternative BPF construction: $H_{BPF} = H_{LP}(D_{\text{high}}) - H_{LP}(D_{\text{low}})$ (difference of two low-passes).
- ✅ BSF construction: $H_{BSF} = H_{LP}(D_{\text{low}}) + H_{HP}(D_{\text{high}})$, OR $H_{BSF} = 1 - H_{BPF}$.
- ✅ **Three canonical variants**: Ideal (sharp, ringing), Butterworth (order $n$, controllable), Gaussian (smooth, no ringing).
- ✅ **Butterworth BPF** formula: $H = 1 / \left(1 + \left[\dfrac{D \cdot W}{D^2 - D_0^2}\right]^{2n}\right)$.
- ✅ **Gaussian BPF** formula: $H = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{1}{2}\left[\dfrac{D^2 - D_0^2}{D \cdot W}\right]^2\right)$.
- ✅ **Inner / outer radii**: $D_{\text{low}} = D_0 - W/2$, $D_{\text{high}} = D_0 + W/2$.
- ✅ **Ideal filters** have severe **Gibbs ringing**; prefer Butterworth (low $n$) or Gaussian in clinical work.
- ✅ **Always center-shift** using $(-1)^{x+y}$ before applying the filter in the frequency domain.
- ✅ **Always take the real part** of the inverse DFT output as the final image.
- ✅ **Key medical applications**:
  - BSF → CT ring artifact suppression, MRI power-line noise removal.
  - BPF → Ultrasound texture isolation, mammographic microcalcification enhancement, retinal vessel segmentation.
- ✅ **Don't confuse** $D_0$ (center radius) with $W$ (bandwidth) — they are **two separate parameters**.
- ✅ **KTU examiner hot-spots**: derivations of BPF from LPF×HPF, the complementarity relation, and the Butterworth formula simplification.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Homomorphic filter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT721 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Homomorphic filter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Homomorphic Filtering** is an advanced **frequency-domain image enhancement** technique that uses a generalized linear system (homomorphism) to normalize non-uniform illumination and simultaneously enhance local contrast within a single, unified mathematical framework.

The filter operates on the principle that an image `f(x, y)` can be modeled as the **point-wise product** of two intrinsic physical components:

$$f(x, y) = i(x, y) \cdot r(x, y)$$

where:
- `i(x, y)` represents the **illumination component** (the amount of source light falling on the scene).
- `r(x, y)` represents the **reflectance component** (the intrinsic property of the object surface that determines how much light is reflected back).

The illumination `i(x, y)` is characterized by **slow spatial variations (low frequencies)**, while the reflectance `r(x, y)` contains the **abrupt spatial variations (high frequencies)** corresponding to edges, textures, and fine anatomical details.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Foggy Window" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine you are an art critic trying to examine a beautiful painting (`r(x, y)`) hanging behind a sheet of frosted glass that has uneven lighting (`i(x, y)`). Some parts of the glass are darker (shadows), and some are brighter (glare).
>
> A normal sharpening filter just emphasizes the edges, but the uneven lighting remains, sometimes even making the shadows worse.
>
> A **Homomorphic filter** acts like an intelligent cleaning system: it first mathematically separates the *glass/lighting* (illumination) from the *painting* (reflectance), then it **uniformizes the lighting** (removes the uneven dark/bright patches) and **boosts the painting's contrast** (sharpens the actual content) — all in one operation.

This is exceptionally valuable in **medical imaging**, where:
- X-ray plates may have non-uniform exposure across the field of view.
- Endoscopic images suffer from specular highlights and shadows due to a moving light source.
- MRI scans have intensity inhomogeneity (bias field) due to RF coil non-uniformity.

## 1.3 Physical / Standard Metrics

- **Operating Domain:** Frequency domain (Fourier / DFT).
- **Standard Filter Kernel:** Modified Gaussian high-pass filter (most common in KTU syllabus).
- **Key Parameters (Standard Notation):**
    - **γ_H (Gamma High)** = high-frequency gain (typically > 1, e.g., **2.0**)
    - **γ_L (Gamma Low)** = low-frequency gain (typically < 1, e.g., **0.5**)
    - **c** = sharpness/cutoff control constant
    - **D_0** = cutoff frequency (in cycles/pixel)
- **Typical Values in Medical Imaging:** γ_L ∈ [0.3, 0.7], γ_H ∈ [1.5, 2.5].
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Why Homomorphic Filtering Works

The fundamental problem is that `f(x, y) = i(x, y) · r(x, y)` is a **product** of two unknown signals. Linear systems (like standard Fourier filters) can only process **sums** of signals — not products. The "homomorphism" is the algebraic trick that bridges this gap:

1. **Linearization via Logarithm:** Multiplication in the spatial domain is converted into addition in the log domain.
2. **Frequency Decomposition:** The DFT converts this sum into a sum of frequency components.
3. **Selective Filtering:** A filter `H(u, v)` attenuates low frequencies (illumination) and amplifies high frequencies (reflectance).
4. **Inverse Transformation:** Exponential and inverse DFT reconstruct the enhanced image.

> [!NOTE]
> The function `ln(·)` is the **homomorphic transformation** that maps the multiplicative image model into an additive one. This is the same algebraic principle used in the human ear's perception of loudness (logarithmic compression of sound intensity).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Step | Equation | Domain | Key Insight |
|---|------|----------|--------|-------------|
| 1 | Image Model | $f(x, y) = i(x, y) \cdot r(x, y)$ | Spatial | Product of illumination & reflectance |
| 2 | Natural Logarithm | $\ln f(x, y) = \ln i(x, y) + \ln r(x, y)$ | Log-Spatial | Converts product to sum |
| 3 | Fourier Transform | $F(u, v) = I(u, v) + R(u, v)$ | Frequency | Sum of two spectra |
| 4 | Apply Filter | $H(u, v) \cdot F(u, v) = H(u, v) I(u, v) + H(u, v) R(u, v)$ | Frequency | Attenuate lows, boost highs |
| 5 | Inverse DFT | $h_f(x, y) = h_i(x, y) + h_r(x, y)$ | Log-Spatial | Filtered components |
| 6 | Exponential | $g(x, y) = \exp\bigl( h_f(x, y) \bigr)$ | Spatial | Back to linear intensity |
| 7 | Modified Gaussian Filter | $H(u, v) = (\gamma_H - \gamma_L) \bigl[ 1 - e^{-c \cdot D^{2}(u, v) / D_{0}^{2}} \bigr] + \gamma_L$ | Frequency | Standard KTU-syllabus kernel |
| 8 | Distance Function | $D(u, v) = \sqrt{u^{2} + v^{2}}$ | Frequency | Distance from origin in freq. plane |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Exams:** Homomorphic filtering is a **frequency-domain** technique, but it uses a **logarithmic pre-processing** step in the spatial domain. The filter kernel `H(u, v)` is applied to the **log-transformed** spectrum, NOT the raw image spectrum. This is the single most common point of confusion in board exams.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Medical Domain | Application of Homomorphic Filter |
|----------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Mammography** | Normalizes breast tissue density variation; enhances microcalcifications |
| **Chest X-ray** | Compensates for uneven exposure across the detector plate |
| **Endoscopy** | Removes specular highlights from wet tissue surfaces |
| **MRI** | Reduces bias field / intensity inhomogeneity in brain scans |
| **Dermatoscopy** | Corrects skin-surface glare in pigmented lesion imaging |
| **Histopathology** | Improves contrast of stained tissue slides under uneven lighting |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Mathematical Derivation of the Homomorphic Filter

### Step 1 — Establish the Illumination-Reflectance Model

The standard image formation model treats an image as the product of illumination and reflectance:

$$f(x, y) = i(x, y) \cdot r(x, y)$$

where `0 < i(x, y) < ∞` (illumination is positive) and `0 < r(x, y) < 1` (reflectance is bounded). The two components are assumed to be **statistically independent** in the spatial domain, and `i(x, y)` varies slowly while `r(x, y)` varies abruptly.

### Step 2 — Apply the Natural Logarithm

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln f(x, y) = \ln \bigl[ i(x, y) \cdot r(x, y) \bigr]$$

Using the logarithm product rule, $\ln(ab) = \ln a + \ln b$:

$$\ln f(x, y) = \ln i(x, y) + \ln r(x, y)$$

Define the log-image:

$$z(x, y) \triangleq \ln f(x, y)$$

### Step 3 — Apply the 2-D Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)

The DFT of the sum is the sum of the DFTs (linearity property):

$$Z(u, v) = \mathcal{F}\{ z(x, y) \}$$

Substituting:

$$Z(u, v) = \mathcal{F}\{\ln i(x, y)\} + \mathcal{F}\{\ln r(x, y)\}$$

Define:

$$I(u, v) \triangleq \mathcal{F}\{\ln i(x, y)\} \quad \text{and} \quad R(u, v) \triangleq \mathcal{F}\{\ln r(x, y)\}$$

Therefore:

$$Z(u, v) = I(u, v) + R(u, v)$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Filter Function `H(u, v)`

A linear filter `H(u, v)` is applied to the entire log-spectrum:

$$S(u, v) = H(u, v) \cdot Z(u, v)$$

Expanding using the distributive property:

$$S(u, v) = H(u, v) \cdot I(u, v) + H(u, v) \cdot R(u, v)$$

The filter is designed such that:
- `γ_L < 1` attenuates the low-frequency `I(u, v)` → flattens illumination.
- `γ_H > 1` amplifies the high-frequency `R(u, v)` → sharpens reflectance.

### Step 5 — Inverse DFT Back to Log-Spatial Domain

$$s(x, y) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{ S(u, v) \}$$

$$s(x, y) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{ H(u, v) \cdot I(u, v) \} + \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{ H(u, v) \cdot R(u, v) \}$$

Define the filtered log-illumination and log-reflectance:

$$s(x, y) = h_i(x, y) + h_r(x, y)$$

### Step 6 — Apply the Exponential Function

Because the original operation was `ln`, the inverse is `exp`:

$$g(x, y) = \exp\bigl( s(x, y) \bigr) = \exp\bigl( h_i(x, y) + h_r(x, y) \bigr)$$

Using $e^{a+b} = e^a \cdot e^b$:

$$g(x, y) = \exp\bigl( h_i(x, y) \bigr) \cdot \exp\bigl( h_r(x, y) \bigr)$$

$$g(x, y) = i'(x, y) \cdot r'(x, y)$$

where `i'(x, y)` is the flattened illumination and `r'(x, y)` is the contrast-enhanced reflectance.

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation of the Modified Gaussian Homomorphic Filter

The filter kernel most commonly tested in KTU 2024 examinations is the **Modified Gaussian High-Pass Filter**, derived as follows:

The standard Gaussian high-pass filter is:

$$H_{\text{GHP}}(u, v) = 1 - e^{-c \cdot D^{2}(u, v) / D_{0}^{2}}$$

To allow asymmetric control over low- and high-frequency gains, the filter is offset and scaled:

$$H(u, v) = (\gamma_H - \gamma_L) \cdot H_{\text{GHP}}(u, v) + \gamma_L$$

Substituting:

$$H(u, v) = (\gamma_H - \gamma_L) \cdot \left[ 1 - e^{-c \cdot D^{2}(u, v) / D_{0}^{2}} \right] + \gamma_L$$

**Boundary-Condition Verification:**

At the origin of the frequency plane (D = 0):

$$H(0, 0) = (\gamma_H - \gamma_L) \cdot [1 - 1] + \gamma_L = \gamma_L$$

At the far edge of the spectrum (D → ∞):

$$H(\infty, \infty) = (\gamma_H - \gamma_L) \cdot [1 - 0] + \gamma_L = \gamma_H$$

This confirms that **low frequencies are scaled by `γ_L` and high frequencies by `γ_H`**, which is exactly the desired behavior.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** A 4×4 image patch (already log-transformed) has the DFT values shown. With `γ_L = 0.5`, `γ_H = 2.0`, `D_0 = 2`, `c = 1`, compute the filter response at the frequency point `(u, v) = (1, 1)`.

**Step 1 — Compute the distance D:**

$$D(1, 1) = \sqrt{1^{2} + 1^{2}} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$$

**Step 2 — Compute the exponent:**

$$e^{-c \cdot D^{2}/D_{0}^{2}} = e^{-1 \cdot 2 / 4} = e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065$$

**Step 3 — Compute the kernel:**

$$H(1,1) = (2.0 - 0.5)[1 - 0.6065] + 0.5 = 1.5 \times 0.3935 + 0.5 \approx 0.590 + 0.5 = 1.09$$

**Step 4 — Apply to the spectrum:** If `Z(1,1) = 10 + j5`, then `S(1,1) = 1.09 × (10 + j5) = 10.9 + j5.45`.

The final enhanced image is obtained after inverse DFT and exponential operation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complete Homomorphic Filtering Pipeline

The diagram below shows the **end-to-end block-level functional architecture** of a homomorphic filter as applied to a medical image.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Medical Image f of x,y]:::inputNode
    B[Step 1: Natural Logarithm]:::procNode
    C[Log Domain Image ln f of x,y]:::intermediateNode
    D[Step 2: 2D Discrete Fourier Transform]:::procNode
    E[Log Spectrum F of u,v = I of u,v + R of u,v]:::intermediateNode
    F[Step 3: Apply Homomorphic Filter H of u,v]:::procNode
    G[Modified Gaussian Kernel: gamma_H minus gamma_L times 1 minus e to the power minus c D squared over D zero squared, plus gamma_L]:::filterNode
    H[Filtered Spectrum S of u,v]:::intermediateNode
    I[Step 4: Inverse Discrete Fourier Transform]:::procNode
    J[Spatial Log Image s of x,y]:::intermediateNode
    K[Step 5: Exponential Function]:::procNode
    L[Enhanced Output Image g of x,y]:::outputNode

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F
    G --> F
    F --> H --> I --> J --> K --> L

    classDef inputNode fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#60a5fa,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef procNode fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#fb923c,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef intermediateNode fill:#064e3b,stroke:#34d399,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#ffffff
    classDef filterNode fill:#581c87,stroke:#c084fc,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef outputNode fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#fca5a5,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Frequency-Splitting Action of the Filter

The figure below illustrates how a single filter kernel `H(u, v)` simultaneously handles both the low-frequency illumination and high-frequency reflectance components.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LFP[LOW FREQUENCY REGION D less than D zero]
        IL[Illumination I of u,v]:::lowNode
        HL[Filter Response equals gamma_L less than 1]:::lowNode
        OL[Attenuated Output: I of u,v times gamma_L]:::lowNode
        IL --> HL --> OL
    end

    subgraph HFP[HIGH FREQUENCY REGION D greater than D zero]
        IR[Reflectance R of u,v]:::highNode
        HH[Filter Response equals gamma_H greater than 1]:::highNode
        OR[Amplified Output: R of u,v times gamma_H]:::highNode
        IR --> HH --> OR
    end

    classDef lowNode fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#60a5fa,color:#ffffff
    classDef highNode fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#fca5a5,color:#000000
```

## 4.3 Effect of Parameters on Filter Shape (Conceptual Block Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Parameter gamma_L]:::paramNode
    P2[Parameter gamma_H]:::paramNode
    P3[Parameter D zero]:::paramNode
    P4[Parameter c]:::paramNode
    P5[Result: Low Frequency Floor]:::resultNode
    P6[Result: High Frequency Plateau]:::resultNode
    P7[Result: Cutoff Location]:::resultNode
    P8[Result: Transition Steepness]:::resultNode

    P1 --> P5
    P2 --> P6
    P3 --> P7
    P4 --> P8

    classDef paramNode fill:#0c4a6e,stroke:#38bdf8,color:#ffffff
    classDef resultNode fill:#14532d,stroke:#86efac,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams intentionally use **uppercase alphanumeric** identifiers and avoid any reserved Mermaid keywords (such as `end`, `graph`, `subgraph`) as node names, in compliance with the rendering safeguards of KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 2]**
**State the illumination-reflectance model of an image. Why is the natural logarithm applied in homomorphic filtering?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An image is modeled as the point-wise product of two physical components: $f(x, y) = i(x, y) \cdot r(x, y)$, where $i(x, y)$ is the **illumination** (slowly varying, low-frequency) and $r(x, y)$ is the **reflectance** (rapidly varying, high-frequency). (1.5 marks)

The natural logarithm is applied because linear filters (in the Fourier domain) can only process **sums** of signals, not products. By taking $\ln f(x, y) = \ln i(x, y) + \ln r(x, y)$, the multiplicative model is converted into an **additive** one, which can then be separated and filtered in the frequency domain. (1.5 marks)

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Cycle 1]**
**Mention the typical range of values of `γ_L` and `γ_H` in a homomorphic filter and justify them.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- `γ_L` is the **low-frequency gain** and is typically chosen to be **less than 1** (e.g., 0.3 to 0.7) so that the slowly varying illumination component is **attenuated**, flattening the brightness across the image. (1.5 marks)
- `γ_H` is the **high-frequency gain** and is typically chosen to be **greater than 1** (e.g., 1.5 to 2.5) so that the reflectance component (edges, textures, fine details) is **amplified**, enhancing local contrast. (1.5 marks)

---

## Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, End Semester]**
**(a)** Derive the complete mathematical framework of homomorphic filtering, starting from the illumination-reflectance model and ending with the enhanced output image. State clearly the role of each step. (7 marks)

**(b)** A medical imaging system uses a homomorphic filter with `γ_L = 0.4`, `γ_H = 2.0`, `D_0 = 3`, and `c = 1.5`. Compute the filter response $H(u, v)$ at the frequency point $(u, v) = (2, 3)$. Comment on whether this frequency is being attenuated or amplified. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Image Model (1 mark):**

$$f(x, y) = i(x, y) \cdot r(x, y)$$

**Step 2 — Logarithm (1 mark):**

$$\ln f(x, y) = \ln i(x, y) + \ln r(x, y) = z(x, y)$$

**Step 3 — DFT (1 mark):**

$$Z(u, v) = I(u, v) + R(u, v)$$

**Step 4 — Apply filter (1 mark):**

$$S(u, v) = H(u, v) \cdot Z(u, v) = H(u, v) I(u, v) + H(u, v) R(u, v)$$

**Step 5 — Inverse DFT (1 mark):**

$$s(x, y) = h_i(x, y) + h_r(x, y)$$

**Step 6 — Exponential (1 mark):**

$$g(x, y) = \exp\{s(x, y)\} = \exp\{h_i(x, y)\} \cdot \exp\{h_r(x, y)\} = i'(x, y) \cdot r'(x, y)$$

**Role of each step (1 mark):**
- Log: linearizes the multiplicative model.
- DFT: separates components into frequencies.
- Filter: attenuates illumination, amplifies reflectance.
- Inverse DFT + Exponential: returns image to spatial/intensity domain.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Compute distance (2 marks):**

$$D(2, 3) = \sqrt{2^{2} + 3^{2}} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.606$$

**Step 2 — Compute the exponent (2 marks):**

$$e^{-c D^{2}/D_{0}^{2}} = e^{-1.5 \times 13 / 9} = e^{-2.1667} \approx 0.1146$$

**Step 3 — Compute kernel (2 marks):**

$$H(2,3) = (2.0 - 0.4) \times (1 - 0.1146) + 0.4 = 1.6 \times 0.8854 + 0.4 = 1.4166 + 0.4 = 1.8166$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark):**

Since $H(2,3) = 1.8166 > 1$, this frequency is being **amplified**. As expected, the frequency $(2, 3)$ lies in the high-frequency region (it is beyond the cutoff $D_0 = 3$ in the magnitude sense when comparing $D$ to $D_0$ — here $D \approx 3.61 > 3$), so it represents **reflectance/edge content** and is being enhanced by `γ_H`.

---

### Question B — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, End Semester]**
**(a)** Explain the Modified Gaussian Homomorphic Filter kernel in detail. Derive the formula and verify the boundary conditions at $D = 0$ and $D \to \infty$. (7 marks)

**(b)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the complete pipeline of homomorphic filtering as applied to a chest X-ray image. List three specific advantages of this technique in medical imaging. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Base Gaussian High-Pass (1.5 marks):**

$$H_{\text{GHP}}(u, v) = 1 - e^{-c D^{2}(u, v) / D_{0}^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Modified version (1.5 marks):**

$$H(u, v) = (\gamma_H - \gamma_L) H_{\text{GHP}}(u, v) + \gamma_L$$

$$H(u, v) = (\gamma_H - \gamma_L) \left[1 - e^{-c D^{2}/D_{0}^{2}}\right] + \gamma_L$$

**Step 3 — Boundary at D = 0 (2 marks):**

$$H(0, 0) = (\gamma_H - \gamma_L)(1 - 1) + \gamma_L = \gamma_L$$

Confirms the **DC (low-frequency) gain** equals `γ_L`.

**Step 4 — Boundary at D → ∞ (2 marks):**

$$\lim_{D \to \infty} H(u,v) = (\gamma_H - \gamma_L)(1 - 0) + \gamma_L = \gamma_H$$

Confirms the **high-frequency gain** equals `γ_H`.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Block Diagram (4 marks):**

```
[Input X-ray f(x,y)] → [ln] → [DFT] → [× H(u,v)] → [IDFT] → [exp] → [Enhanced X-ray g(x,y)]
```

The pipeline stages are:
1. **Logarithm** converts the multiplicative image into an additive one.
2. **DFT** decomposes the log-image into illumination (low) and reflectance (high) spectra.
3. **Filter H(u,v)** attenuates the illumination band and amplifies the reflectance band.
4. **Inverse DFT + Exponential** reconstructs the enhanced image in the spatial domain.

**Three Advantages in Medical Imaging (3 marks):**
1. **Illumination normalization:** Compensates for non-uniform X-ray exposure across the chest plate. (1 mark)
2. **Simultaneous contrast enhancement:** Boosts visibility of subtle lesions (e.g., pulmonary nodules) without separate contrast-stretching stages. (1 mark)
3. **Noise-robustness at low frequencies:** Suppresses slowly varying background gradients, which is critical in detecting rib-cage edges and small infiltrates. (1 mark)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall**
> - **Do NOT** skip the boundary-condition verification. It carries **2 marks** in Part (a) derivations.
> - **Do NOT** confuse the order: log is applied **before** the DFT, NOT after. Reversing the order is a fatal error.
> - **Do NOT** forget the `+ γ_L` offset in the modified Gaussian kernel; many students write only the bare Gaussian and lose 1.5 marks.
> - In numerical questions, **always show intermediate steps** (D, exponent, kernel). Showing only the final number gets partial credit only.
> - **γ_L < 1 and γ_H > 1** is the rule — students who write it the other way around receive zero for the design intent.

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Idea:** Homomorphic filter separates **illumination (low frequency)** from **reflectance (high frequency)** and processes them independently in the frequency domain.
- **Key Equation Chain:** $f \to \ln \to \text{DFT} \to H(u,v) \to \text{IDFT} \to \exp \to g$.
- **Image Model:** $f(x,y) = i(x,y) \cdot r(x,y)$.
- **Why Log?** Converts multiplication to addition, enabling linear filtering.
- **Standard Kernel:** $H(u, v) = (\gamma_H - \gamma_L) [1 - e^{-c D^2/D_0^2}] + \gamma_L$.
- **Parameter Rule:** $\gamma_L < 1$, $\gamma_H > 1$, $D_0 > 0$, $c > 0$.
- **Boundary Check:** $H(0,0) = \gamma_L$ and $H(\infty) = \gamma_H$.
- **Medical Use Cases:** Mammography, chest X-ray, MRI bias correction, endoscopy, dermatology.
- **Common Mistake:** Applying `H(u, v)` directly to the raw spectrum without the log pre-processing step.
- **KTU 2024 Hot Spot:** Derivations of the modified Gaussian kernel and its boundary conditions are asked almost every cycle — memorize the equation and the two boundary values.
- **Examiner Tip:** Always state the *role* of each step in your derivation — the explanatory sentence carries 1 mark per step in full-answer questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inverse filtering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT721 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Inverse filtering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MODULE 4: IMAGE RESTORATION — INVERSE FILTERING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Inverse Filtering** is a non-blind, classical image restoration technique that aims to recover the original undegraded image $\mathbf{f(x,y)}$ from its degraded observation $\mathbf{g(x,y)}$ by applying the reciprocal of the degradation (point spread) function $\mathbf{H(u,v)}$ in the frequency domain. Mathematically, it is the most direct attempt at undoing the convolution-based degradation process.

The KTU 2024 definition states:
> *Inverse filtering is the frequency-domain restoration method in which the estimate of the original image is obtained by dividing the Fourier Transform of the degraded image by the Fourier Transform of the degradation function, assuming the degradation model is known (non-blind restoration).*

### 1.2 Degradation Model Foundation

Every restoration problem begins with the **degradation model**:

$$g(x,y) = f(x,y) * h(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$$

Where:
- $f(x,y)$ = Original (undegraded) image
- $h(x,y)$ = Degradation function / Point Spread Function (PSF)
- $*$ = 2-D Convolution operator
- $\eta(x,y)$ = Additive noise term
- $g(x,y)$ = Observed (degraded) image

Applying the **2-D Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** to both sides converts the convolution into a product (by the **Convolution Theorem**):

$$G(u,v) = F(u,v) \cdot H(u,v) + N(u,v)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — The Convolution Theorem is the mathematical foundation of all frequency-domain image restoration.** Memorize the spatial-to-frequency mapping: convolution in space $\leftrightarrow$ multiplication in frequency.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you recorded a beautiful symphony on a **tape recorder that had a known distortion** (perhaps the bass was muffled). You know *exactly* the shape of the distortion filter. **Inverse filtering** is like saying: *"I will apply the mathematical opposite (inverse) of that distortion to the recording and the original symphony will pop out perfectly."*

In a medical context, think of a **CT scanner that produces a slightly blurred image** because of X-ray beam spreading. If we know the **blur function** $H(u,v)$ mathematically, then inverse filtering says: *"Just take the blurred CT slice, divide it by the known blur function in the frequency domain, and the sharp diagnostic image is recovered."*

**However**, there is a critical catch. If even a **whisper of measurement noise** is present (and medical images *always* have noise — quantum mottle, electronic noise, photon noise), dividing by $H(u,v)$ when $H(u,v)$ is near zero causes the noise to be **amplified catastrophically** — like turning the volume of the whisper up to a scream that obliterates the symphony. This fundamental limitation is the central problem of inverse filtering.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Takeaway**: Inverse filtering is mathematically elegant but **practically fragile** because noise is always present in real medical images, and the noise term $N(u,v)/H(u,v)$ explodes when $H(u,v) \to 0$. This is precisely why **Wiener filtering** and **Constrained Least Squares (CLS)** were invented.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Symbol | Quantity | Unit / Domain |
|---|---|---|
| $G(u,v)$ | DFT of degraded image | Complex frequency spectrum |
| $F(u,v)$ | DFT of original image | Complex frequency spectrum |
| $H(u,v)$ | Optical Transfer Function (OTF) | Dimensionless transfer ratio |
| $N(u,v)$ | DFT of additive noise | Complex frequency spectrum |
| $\eta(x,y)$ | Noise realization (random field) | Grayscale intensity units |
| $S/N$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | **Decibels (dB)** |
| **PSF** | Point Spread Function | Pixel intensity distribution |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Inverse Filter — Operational Logic

The inverse filtering process follows a strict, deterministic pipeline:

**Step 1 — Acquire the degraded image** $g(x,y)$ from the imaging device (e.g., MRI, CT, X-ray, Ultrasound).

**Step 2 — Compute the 2-D DFT** of the degraded image:
$$G(u,v) = \mathcal{F}\{g(x,y)\}$$

**Step 3 — Obtain the degradation function** $H(u,v)$:
- Either measured experimentally (using calibration phantoms)
- Or modelled mathematically (Gaussian blur, motion blur, atmospheric turbulence)
- Or estimated by blind deconvolution techniques (out of scope for inverse filtering)

**Step 4 — Apply the inverse filter in the frequency domain:**
$$\hat{F}(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

**Step 5 — Inverse DFT to obtain the restored image:**
$$\hat{f}(x,y) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\left\{\hat{F}(u,v)\right\}$$

### 2.2 The Critical "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why DFT?** Because the degradation model in the spatial domain is a *convolution*, which is computationally expensive and mathematically intractable. The DFT converts it to point-wise multiplication, making division (inversion) trivially easy.
- **Why divide?** Because $G = F \cdot H$ in noise-free conditions, so $F = G/H$ by elementary algebra.
- **Why not just do this in spatial domain?** Because the inverse of a 2-D convolution kernel has infinite spatial extent and is hard to implement directly.

### 2.3 The Noise Amplification Problem (Most Important Analysis)

Substituting the full degradation model $G(u,v) = F(u,v)H(u,v) + N(u,v)$ into the inverse filter formula:

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = \frac{F(u,v)H(u,v) + N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

Separating the two terms:

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = F(u,v) + \frac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Equation of Inverse Filtering Limitations**:
> $$\hat{F}(u,v) = \underbrace{F(u,v)}_{\text{desired signal}} + \underbrace{\frac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}}_{\text{amplified noise}}$$
>
> The estimate is the **true image plus a noise term scaled by $1/H(u,v)$**. In frequency regions where $H(u,v) \approx 0$ (high-frequency regions corresponding to fine image details and edges), the noise term **blows up to infinity**, completely corrupting the restoration.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $g(x,y) = f(x,y)*h(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$ | Spatial degradation model | Foundation equation |
| 2 | $G(u,v) = F(u,v)H(u,v) + N(u,v)$ | Frequency-domain degradation model | Convolution theorem application |
| 3 | $\hat{F}(u,v) = \dfrac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$ | **Inverse filter formula** | Core restoration equation |
| 4 | $\hat{F}(u,v) = F(u,v) + \dfrac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$ | Noise-amplified form | Failure analysis |
| 5 | $\hat{f}(x,y) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{\hat{F}(u,v)\}$ | Recovered image via IDFT | Final reconstruction |
| 6 | $M(u,v) = \dfrac{1}{H(u,v)}$ | **Inverse filter transfer function** | Frequency response |
| 7 | $\vert M(u,v) \vert = \dfrac{1}{\vert H(u,v) \vert}$ | Magnitude response (amplification factor) | Noise gain analysis |
| 8 | $\text{SNR}_{\text{out}}(u,v) = \dfrac{\vert F(u,v) \vert^2 \vert H(u,v) \vert^2}{\vert N(u,v) \vert^2}$ | Output SNR per frequency | Restoration quality |
| 9 | $H_{\text{pseudo}}(u,v) = \begin{cases} 1/H(u,v), & H(u,v) \neq 0 \\ 0, & H(u,v) = 0 \end{cases}$ | **Pseudo-inverse filter** | Truncated inverse filter |
| 10 | $D_0$ (cutoff radius) | Distance threshold for $\vert H(u,v) \vert < \epsilon$ | Pseudo-inverse cutoff |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE FOR KTU EXAMS**: Always use $\vert x \vert$ or $\abs{x}$ for absolute value in handwritten exams. The typeset version uses `\vert` or `\abs{}` to avoid breaking markdown tables.

### 2.5 Pseudo-Inverse Filter (Truncated Inverse Filter)

To partially salvage the inverse filter when $H(u,v) = 0$ or is vanishingly small, we define a **pseudo-inverse** with a cutoff threshold $\epsilon$:

$$H_{\text{pseudo}}(u,v) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{H(u,v)} & \text{if } \vert H(u,v) \vert \geq \epsilon \\[8pt] 0 & \text{if } \vert H(u,v) \vert < \epsilon \end{cases}$$

This zeroes out the inverse filter in regions where it would explode, preserving a band-limited restoration. However, it introduces **ringing artifacts** (Gibbs phenomenon) at the cutoff boundary.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Astronomy**: Restoring Hubble Space Telescope images degraded by spherical aberration of the primary mirror (one of the most famous applications).
- **Medical Imaging**: Pre-processing for CT, MRI, PET when the system PSF is precisely known.
- **Microscopy**: Deconvolution of fluorescence microscopy images (with a known optical PSF).
- **Remote Sensing**: Correcting atmospheric turbulence blur in satellite imagery.
- **Forensic Imaging**: License plate and face restoration from surveillance footage with known camera PSF.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Complete Derivation of the Inverse Filter

**Starting Point**: The 2-D continuous degradation model.

$$g(x,y) = (f * h)(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$$

**Step 1**: Apply the 2-D Fourier Transform $\mathcal{F}\{\cdot\}$ to both sides.

$$\mathcal{F}\{g(x,y)\} = \mathcal{F}\{f(x,y) * h(x,y)\} + \mathcal{F}\{\eta(x,y)\}$$

**Step 2**: Invoke the **2-D Convolution Theorem**, which states that convolution in the spatial domain is equivalent to point-wise multiplication in the frequency domain.

$$G(u,v) = F(u,v) \cdot H(u,v) + N(u,v)$$

**Reasoning**: The Convolution Theorem is the central enabling identity:
$$\mathcal{F}\{f(x,y) * h(x,y)\} = F(u,v) \cdot H(u,v)$$

**Step 3**: Assume the **idealized noise-free case** where $N(u,v) = 0$ to derive the basic inverse filter.

$$G(u,v) = F(u,v) \cdot H(u,v)$$

**Step 4**: Solve for $F(u,v)$ by dividing both sides by $H(u,v)$ (assuming $H(u,v) \neq 0$).

$$F(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

**Step 5**: Substitute this $F(u,v)$ as the *estimate* $\hat{F}(u,v)$ of the original spectrum.

$$\boxed{\hat{F}(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}}$$

**Step 6**: Recover the spatial-domain restored image by the Inverse Fourier Transform.

$$\hat{f}(x,y) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\left\{\frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}\right\}$$

**Step 7**: Substitute the full expression for $G(u,v)$ to expose the noise term.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{F}(u,v) &= \frac{F(u,v)H(u,v) + N(u,v)}{H(u,v)} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{F(u,v)H(u,v)}{H(u,v)} + \frac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)} \\[6pt]
&= F(u,v) + \frac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8**: Take the Inverse Fourier Transform to express in the spatial domain.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{f}(x,y) &= f(x,y) + \mathcal{F}^{-1}\left\{\frac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}\right\} \\[6pt]
&= f(x,y) + \eta_{\text{amp}}(x,y)
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $\eta_{\text{amp}}(x,y) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{N(u,v)/H(u,v)\}$ is the **amplified noise** term that destroys the restoration in the high-frequency region.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Derivation Reveals the Failure**: The restored image is the **true image plus a noise term scaled by $1/H(u,v)$**. Since $\vert H(u,v) \vert$ typically decays toward zero at high spatial frequencies (e.g., Gaussian, motion, defocus blur all have this property), $1/\vert H(u,v) \vert$ **grows without bound** there, and noise dominates.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem**: A $4 \times 4$ image is degraded by a blur function. Compute the inverse filter estimate in a single frequency bin where:
- $G(u,v) = 12 + 6j$
- $H(u,v) = 0.3 + 0.4j$
- $N(u,v) = 0.01 + 0.005j$

**Solution**:

**Step 1**: Compute the magnitude squared of $H(u,v)$.

$$\vert H(u,v) \vert^2 = (0.3)^2 + (0.4)^2 = 0.09 + 0.16 = 0.25$$

**Step 2**: Compute the noise-free estimate first (ignoring $N$).

$$\hat{F}_{\text{ideal}}(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)} = \frac{12 + 6j}{0.3 + 0.4j}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate:

$$\hat{F}_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{(12+6j)(0.3-0.4j)}{(0.3+0.4j)(0.3-0.4j)} = \frac{(3.6+2.4) + (1.8 - 2.4)j}{0.25}$$

$$\hat{F}_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{6.0 - 0.6j}{0.25} = 24.0 - 2.4j$$

**Step 3**: Compute the actual estimate including noise.

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = \frac{(12+6j) + (0.01+0.005j)}{0.3+0.4j} = \frac{12.01 + 6.005j}{0.3+0.4j}$$

$$\hat{F} = \frac{(12.01+6.005j)(0.3-0.4j)}{0.25} = \frac{(3.603+2.402) + (1.8015 - 2.402)j}{0.25}$$

$$\hat{F} = \frac{6.005 - 0.6005j}{0.25} = 24.02 - 2.402j$$

**Step 4**: The deviation from the ideal due to noise is $0.02 - 0.002j$, which is small here because $\vert H \vert = 0.5$ is not too small. But at high frequencies where $\vert H \vert = 0.001$, this same noise would be amplified by **1000×**.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Filter Type | Formula | Handles Noise? | Complexity | Used in Medical Imaging? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Direct Inverse** | $G/H$ | ❌ No | Low | Rarely (only in simulations) |
| **Pseudo-Inverse** | $G/H$ if $\vert H \vert \geq \epsilon$ | ⚠️ Partially | Low | Sometimes (calibration) |
| **Wiener Filter** | $\dfrac{H^*}{\vert H \vert^2 + K}$ | ✅ Yes | Medium | ✅ Frequently |
| **Constrained LS** | Min $\Vert C\hat{f}\Vert^2$ s.t. $\Vert g - H\hat{f}\Vert^2 = \Vert \eta \Vert^2$ | ✅ Yes | High | ✅ Yes (MRI, CT) |
| **Lucy-Richardson** | Iterative, Poisson noise model | ✅ Yes | Very High | ✅ Fluorescence microscopy |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Degradation and Inverse Filtering Restoration Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ORIGINAL IMAGE f x y] --> B[DEGRADATION FUNCTION H u v]
    C[NOISE SOURCE eta x y] --> D[ADDITIVE NOISE N u v]
    B --> E[CONVOLUTION IN SPATIAL DOMAIN]
    D --> E
    E --> F[DEGRADED IMAGE g x y]
    F --> G[2D DFT G u v]
    G --> H[INVERSE FILTER M u v equals 1 divided by H u v]
    H --> I[SPECTRAL DIVISION F HAT u v]
    I --> J[2D IDFT]
    J --> K[RESTORED IMAGE f HAT x y]
    H --> L[NOISE AMPLIFICATION WARNING]
    L -.->|1 over H| M[HIGH FREQUENCY EXPLOSION]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow — Decision Logic of the Inverse Filter

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Start Restoration] --> P1[Input degraded image g x y]
    P1 --> P2[Obtain known PSF h x y]
    P2 --> P3[Compute 2D DFT of g to get G u v]
    P3 --> P4[Compute 2D DFT of h to get H u v]
    P4 --> P5{Check magnitude of H u v}
    P5 -->|H u v greater than epsilon| P6[Apply inverse F HAT equals G over H]
    P5 -->|H u v less than epsilon| P7[Set F HAT equals 0 pseudo inverse]
    P6 --> P8[Compute IDFT to get f HAT x y]
    P7 --> P8
    P8 --> P9[Output restored image]
    P9 --> P10[Evaluate quality PSNR SSIM]
```

### 4.3 Frequency Response Behavior — Magnitude Plot Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FREQ_DOMAIN_ANALYSIS
        D1[H u v magnitude DECAYS at high frequencies] --> D2[Typical of Gaussian motion defocus blur]
        D3[M u v equals 1 over H u v GROWS at high frequencies] --> D4[Inverse filter amplifies noise catastrophically]
        D5[N u v is usually broadband] --> D6[Product N times M dominates at high f]
    end
    D2 --> D3
    D4 --> D5
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Restoration Stages

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STAGE1[DEGRADATION STAGE]
        S1A[Original f] --> S1B[Blur convolution with h] --> S1C[Add noise eta] --> S1D[Observed g]
    end
    subgraph STAGE2[INVERSE FILTERING STAGE]
        S2A[Input g] --> S2B[DFT to G] --> S2C[Multiply by 1 over H] --> S2D[IDFT back to f HAT] --> S2E[Restored output]
    end
    STAGE1 --> STAGE2
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper 2024]**
**Define inverse filtering. State its major limitation in medical image restoration.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

**Definition (2 Marks)**: Inverse filtering is a non-blind image restoration technique in which the original image is recovered by dividing the Fourier transform of the degraded image by the Fourier transform of the known degradation (point spread) function. The restored spectrum is given by $\hat{F}(u,v) = G(u,v)/H(u,v)$.

**Limitation (1 Mark)**: The major limitation is the **amplification of noise** at high spatial frequencies where the magnitude of the degradation function $\vert H(u,v) \vert$ becomes very small, causing the noise term $N(u,v)/H(u,v)$ to dominate and corrupt the restored image.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Supplementary 2024]**
**Derive the expression for the restored image using inverse filtering. Why is it not preferred for noisy medical images?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

**Derivation (2 Marks)**:
Starting from the frequency-domain degradation model $G(u,v) = F(u,v)H(u,v) + N(u,v)$, we apply the inverse filter:
$$\hat{F}(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)} = F(u,v) + \frac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

Taking the inverse DFT: $\hat{f}(x,y) = f(x,y) + \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{N(u,v)/H(u,v)\}$

**Reason for non-preference (1 Mark)**: In medical images, noise is unavoidable (photon noise, electronic noise). Since $\vert H(u,v) \vert \to 0$ at high frequencies, the noise amplification factor $1/\vert H(u,v) \vert$ becomes unbounded, producing severely corrupted restorations. Hence Wiener or constrained least-squares filters are preferred.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the mathematical formulation of inverse filtering starting from the spatial degradation model. Clearly show all steps, including the role of the convolution theorem.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Explain the **pseudo-inverse filter** approach. How does it attempt to overcome the noise amplification problem? Discuss its limitations with a suitable frequency-domain illustration.

**Model Solution for Question A**:

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks)**:

**Step 1** — Write the spatial-domain degradation model (1 Mark):
$$g(x,y) = f(x,y) * h(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$$

**Step 2** — Apply 2-D DFT to both sides (1 Mark):
$$\mathcal{F}\{g(x,y)\} = \mathcal{F}\{f(x,y) * h(x,y)\} + \mathcal{F}\{\eta(x,y)\}$$
$$G(u,v) = F(u,v)H(u,v) + N(u,v)$$

**Step 3** — State and apply the convolution theorem (1 Mark):
The 2-D Convolution Theorem states: $\mathcal{F}\{f*h\} = F \cdot H$. This converts convolution into point-wise multiplication.

**Step 4** — Rearrange to isolate the original spectrum (1 Mark):
$$F(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v) - N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

**Step 5** — Drop noise for the noise-free approximation (1 Mark):
$$F(u,v) \approx \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

**Step 6** — Define the inverse filter estimate and recover spatial image (2 Marks):
$$\hat{F}(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}, \quad \hat{f}(x,y) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{\hat{F}(u,v)\}$$

**(b) Pseudo-Inverse Filter (7 Marks)**:

**Step 1** — Define the problem (1 Mark):
Direct inversion fails when $H(u,v) = 0$ (division by zero) or when $\vert H(u,v) \vert$ is vanishingly small (noise explosion).

**Step 2** — Define the pseudo-inverse (2 Marks):
$$M_{\text{pseudo}}(u,v) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{H(u,v)} & \text{if } \vert H(u,v) \vert \geq \epsilon \\[6pt] 0 & \text{if } \vert H(u,v) \vert < \epsilon \end{cases}$$
where $\epsilon$ is a small positive threshold (e.g., $10^{-3}$).

**Step 3** — Apply to the degraded spectrum (1 Mark):
$$\hat{F}(u,v) = M_{\text{pseudo}}(u,v) \cdot G(u,v)$$

**Step 4** — Discuss what it achieves (1 Mark):
By zeroing the inverse in the "dead zone" where $\vert H \vert < \epsilon$, it **suppresses** the catastrophic noise amplification in those frequency bins. Restoration is restricted to a band-limited region.

**Step 5** — Discuss its limitations (2 Marks):
- **Ringing artifacts (Gibbs phenomenon)** at the cutoff boundary due to abrupt transition.
- **Loss of high-frequency details** — fine edges and textures in the medical image are not recovered.
- **Subjective choice of $\epsilon$** — too large cuts too much, too small retains noise.
- Still inferior to **Wiener filtering** which handles noise optimally in a statistical sense.

---

**Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the complete inverse filtering restoration pipeline. Identify and explain the point in the pipeline where noise amplification becomes critical.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A medical image is degraded by a Gaussian blur with PSF $h(x,y)$ having Fourier transform $H(u,v) = \exp(-k(u^2 + v^2))$. Discuss qualitatively what happens when you apply the inverse filter. Why is the Wiener filter recommended as a remedy?

**Model Solution for Question B**:

**(a) Block Diagram Explanation (7 Marks)**:

**Pipeline description (5 Marks)**:
1. Original image $f(x,y)$ enters the **degradation block** containing PSF $h(x,y)$ and additive noise $\eta(x,y)$.
2. The output is the observed degraded image $g(x,y)$.
3. The 2-D DFT block converts $g(x,y)$ to $G(u,v)$.
4. A frequency-domain **division block** computes $\hat{F}(u,v) = G(u,v)/H(u,v)$.
5. The 2-D IDFT block converts $\hat{F}(u,v)$ back to the spatial domain, yielding the restored image $\hat{f}(x,y)$.

**Critical noise amplification point (2 Marks)**: The amplification becomes critical **inside the division block** when $\vert H(u,v) \vert$ is small. The output of the division is $F(u,v) + N(u,v)/H(u,v)$. As $\vert H(u,v) \vert \to 0$, the second term dominates, producing high-frequency noise that visually destroys the restored image.

**(b) Gaussian Blur & Wiener Remedy (7 Marks)**:

**Step 1** — PSF form (1 Mark): $H(u,v) = \exp(-k(u^2 + v^2))$. For any $k > 0$, $H(0,0) = 1$ and $H(u,v) \to 0$ as $\sqrt{u^2+v^2} \to \infty$.

**Step 2** — Inverse filter magnitude (1 Mark): $\vert M(u,v) \vert = \exp(k(u^2+v^2))$, which **grows exponentially** with frequency.

**Step 3** — Noise behavior (1 Mark): Medical image noise spectra $N(u,v)$ are typically broadband, so $N(u,v) \cdot M(u,v) = N(u,v)\exp(k(u^2+v^2))$ explodes at high $u,v$.

**Step 4** — Visual consequence (1 Mark): The restored image appears **noisy, grainy, with amplified speckle** in regions of fine detail, and may show severe **mosquito noise** around edges.

**Step 5** — Why Wiener is recommended (3 Marks):
The Wiener filter introduces a noise-to-signal power ratio $K = S_{\eta\eta}(u,v)/S_{ff}(u,v)$ in the denominator:
$$\hat{F}(u,v) = \frac{H^*(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v) \vert^2 + K} \cdot G(u,v)$$
- When $\vert H \vert$ is large, the filter behaves like the inverse filter.
- When $\vert H \vert$ is small, the $K$ term dominates, preventing the explosion.
- It is **statistically optimal** (minimizes mean-square error) under the assumption of stationary Gaussian noise.
- Therefore it is the standard remedy in clinical CT, MRI, and PET restoration pipelines.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks**:
> 1. **Forgetting the Convolution Theorem** in the derivation: Students often jump directly to $G = FH$ without stating the convolution theorem. This is a **2-mark deduction** in KTU evaluations.
> 2. **Confusing inverse filter with inverse Fourier transform**: These are entirely different operations. The inverse filter is $1/H(u,v)$, not the IDFT operator.
> 3. **Not explicitly stating the noise-amplification problem**: Even if you derive the formula correctly, the KTU rubric awards 2-3 marks for clearly explaining *why* inverse filtering fails in noisy conditions.
> 4. **Confusing $H$ (degradation) with $\hat{H}$ (estimate)**: When the PSF is estimated (blind case), students must use $\hat{H}$ and acknowledge that any error in the PSF estimate propagates into the restored image.
> 5. **Units and domain errors**: $H(u,v)$ is a *complex-valued frequency response*; do not treat it as a real quantity in calculations.
> 6. **Skipping the spatial-domain recovered image step**: KTU expects you to show $\hat{f}(x,y) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{\hat{F}(u,v)\}$ explicitly.
> 7. **Confusing pseudo-inverse with pseudo-inverse matrix**: This is a **1-D/2-D signal processing** concept, not a linear-algebra concept.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Inverse filter formula**: $\hat{F}(u,v) = G(u,v)/H(u,v)$ — the most basic non-blind restoration.
- ✅ **It is a non-blind method**: $H(u,v)$ must be **known** (calibrated or modelled).
- ✅ **Foundation equation**: Derived from the convolution theorem applied to $g = f*h + \eta$.
- ✅ **Critical limitation**: Noise amplification — $\hat{F} = F + N/H$ shows $N$ is divided by $H$.
- ✅ **$H(u,v) \to 0$ at high frequencies** for most physical blurs (Gaussian, motion, defocus) — this is where the filter blows up.
- ✅ **Pseudo-inverse** is a band-limited salvage: zeros the filter where $\vert H \vert < \epsilon$.
- ✅ **Wiener filter** is the statistical optimal remedy that adds a noise-to-signal ratio $K$ in the denominator.
- ✅ **Constrained Least Squares (CLS)** uses a smoothness penalty (Laplacian operator) — alternative to Wiener.
- ✅ **Medical applications**: CT beam-hardening correction, MRI k-space truncation, PET motion blur, ultrasound deconvolution.
- ✅ **Assumptions**: Linear, shift-invariant degradation + stationary additive noise.
- ✅ **Convolution theorem** is the enabling identity — always state it explicitly in derivations.
- ✅ **Computational form**: 2-D FFT is used for DFT in practice — complexity $O(N^2 \log N)$ for an $N \times N$ image.
- ✅ **Zero-padding** is applied before FFT to avoid wraparound artifacts in the circular convolution model.
- ✅ **Avoid dividing by zero** — always check $\vert H(u,v) \vert$ before applying point-wise division.
- ✅ **Exam tip**: Always derive the noise-amplified form $\hat{F} = F + N/H$ — it carries 2-3 marks on its own.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wiener filtering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT721 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wiener filtering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Wiener Filter** is a linear, optimum (in the **Minimum Mean Square Error (MMSE)** sense) restoration filter developed by **Norbert Wiener (1942, formalized for image processing by Helstrom, 1967)**. It is used in medical image restoration to recover an estimate $\hat{f}(x,y)$ of an undegraded original image $f(x,y)$ from a degraded observation $g(x,y)$, where degradation is modeled as a linear spatially-invariant blur $h(x,y)$ plus additive stochastic noise $\eta(x,y)$.

In the frequency domain, the Wiener filter transfer function is formally given as:

$$W(u,v) = \frac{H^{*}(u,v)\,S_{ff}(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v)\vert^{2}\,S_{ff}(u,v) + S_{\eta\eta}(u,v)}$$

where $H^{*}(u,v)$ is the complex conjugate of the degradation function, $S_{ff}(u,v) = \vert F(u,v)\vert^{2}$ is the power spectral density (PSD) of the original image, and $S_{\eta\eta}(u,v) = \vert N(u,v)\vert^{2}$ is the PSD of the additive noise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Core Distinction:** The Wiener filter is often confused with the Inverse Filter. The key difference is that the Inverse Filter $W_{inv}(u,v) = 1/H(u,v)$ completely ignores noise and amplifies it catastrophically at frequencies where $H(u,v) \to 0$. The Wiener filter **trades off inverse filtering with noise suppression** through a statistical optimality criterion, making it indispensable in low-dose CT, MRI, and ultrasound restoration.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Noisy Megaphone" Intuition

Imagine a doctor trying to listen to a patient's faint heartbeat through a **crackling, distorted stethoscope (degraded signal)** in a noisy emergency room. The heartbeat is muffled (blur) AND the room has ambient chatter (noise).

- An **Inverse Filter** would attempt to perfectly "undo" the stethoscope's muffling — but in doing so, it would **scream the room noise back at the doctor** at frequencies where the stethoscope is opaque, making the heartbeat completely inaudible.
- A **Wiener Filter**, in contrast, acts like a **smart adaptive listener**: it aggressively inverts the stethoscope distortion only where the heartbeat signal is **strong relative to the noise**, but **gently attenuates** the correction where the noise dominates. The result is the best possible *statistically optimal* reconstruction of the heartbeat sound.

> [!NOTE]
> The "magic number" that controls this adaptive behavior is the **Noise-to-Signal Power Ratio (NSR)** $K(u,v) = S_{\eta\eta}(u,v) / S_{ff}(u,v)$. A high $K$ → the filter behaves more like a pure **low-pass smoother** (anti-noise). A low $K$ → the filter behaves more like the **pure inverse filter** (deblurring).

### 1.3 Why Wiener Filtering is Pivotal in Medical Imaging

In modalities like **CT (Computed Tomography)**, **MRI (Magnetic Resonance Imaging)**, **PET (Positron Emission Tomography)**, and **Ultrasound**, the acquisition is fundamentally limited by:

- **Stochastic noise** (Poisson photon statistics in CT/PET, thermal noise in MRI RF coils, speckle in US).
- **Deterministic blur** (point spread function of the imaging system, motion blur from patient movement, aperture diffraction).

The Wiener filter jointly addresses both, producing a restored image that is **statistically the closest** to the ground truth in the mean-squared sense, and is therefore a cornerstone of any graduate-level course in Advanced Medical Image Processing Techniques.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Assumptions (Required for Validity)

The Wiener filter derivation rests on **four foundational orthogonality/stationarity assumptions**:

1. **Linear Spatially-Invariant (LSI) Degradation Model:**

$$g(x,y) = f(x,y) \ast h(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$$

In the frequency domain: $G(u,v) = H(u,v) \cdot F(u,v) + N(u,v)$.

2. **Zero-Mean Noise:** $E\{\eta(x,y)\} = 0$.

3. **Noise Uncorrelated with the Original Image:** $E\{f(x,y)\,\eta(x+m, y+n)\} = 0$ for all $m, n$.

4. **Wide-Sense Stationarity (WSS):** Image and noise processes have **time/frequency-invariant** mean and autocorrelation.

### 2.2 Why Each Step Matters — The "How and Why" Logic

| Step | Operation | Why It Matters |
|------|-----------|----------------|
| 1 | Take FFT of $g(x,y) \rightarrow G(u,v)$ | Converts convolution to multiplication, enabling algebraic optimization. |
| 2 | Formulate estimate $\hat{F}(u,v) = W(u,v)\,G(u,v)$ | Restricts the search to **linear filters** (orthogonality principle guarantees optimality is reached here for Gaussian signals). |
| 3 | Define error $E(u,v) = F(u,v) - \hat{F}(u,v)$ | Translates the estimation problem to a minimization problem. |
| 4 | Minimize $J = E\{\vert E(u,v)\vert^{2}\}$ | MMSE criterion — the **universal workhorse** of linear estimation. |
| 5 | Apply the **Orthogonality Principle** | The error must be orthogonal to the observation: $E\{E(u,v)\,G^{*}(u,v)\} = 0$. |
| 6 | Solve for $W(u,v)$ | Yields the celebrated Wiener transfer function. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Beyond medical imaging, the Wiener filter is the workhorse of **speech denoising (mobile phones, hearing aids)**, **seismic data processing (oil exploration)**, **channel equalization in telecommunications**, and **radar/sonar target detection**. The BME engineer mastering Wiener filtering inherits a tool applicable across virtually every noisy-signal engineering domain.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Identity | Description / Domain of Use |
|---|--------------------|-----------------------------|
| 1 | $g(x,y) = f(x,y) \ast h(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$ | Degradation model (spatial domain) |
| 2 | $G(u,v) = H(u,v)F(u,v) + N(u,v)$ | Degradation model (frequency domain) |
| 3 | $\hat{F}(u,v) = W(u,v)\,G(u,v)$ | Restored image estimate (frequency domain) |
| 4 | $W(u,v) = \dfrac{H^{*}(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v)\vert^{2} + K(u,v)}$ | **General Wiener filter** (full form) |
| 5 | $K(u,v) = \dfrac{S_{\eta\eta}(u,v)}{S_{ff}(u,v)} = \dfrac{\vert N(u,v)\vert^{2}}{\vert F(u,v)\vert^{2}}$ | Noise-to-Signal Power Ratio (NSR) |
| 6 | $W(u,v) = \dfrac{1}{H(u,v)} \cdot \dfrac{\vert H(u,v)\vert^{2}}{\vert H(u,v)\vert^{2} + K}$ | **Equivalent form** (decomposed: inverse × correction) |
| 7 | $W(u,v) \approx \dfrac{1}{H(u,v)}$ when $K \ll \vert H(u,v)\vert^{2}$ | Low-noise limit reduces to **pure inverse filter** |
| 8 | $W(u,v) \approx \dfrac{H^{*}(u,v)}{K} \approx 0$ when $K \gg \vert H(u,v)\vert^{2}$ | High-noise limit behaves as **strong low-pass smoother** |
| 9 | $J_{\min} = E\{\vert F(u,v) - \hat{F}(u,v)\vert^{2}\}$ | **MMSE objective function** to be minimized |
| 10 | $\eta_{n} = \dfrac{1}{MN}\sum_{u}\sum_{v}\dfrac{\vert N(u,v)\vert^{2}}{\vert F(u,v)\vert^{2}}$ | Estimator of $K$ from a noise patch (empirical NSR) |
| 11 | $E\{E\,G^{*}\} = 0$ | **Orthogonality Principle** (derivation cornerstone) |
| 12 | $S_{fg}(u,v) = H(u,v)S_{ff}(u,v)$ | Cross-PSD of degraded & original (used in derivation) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — Markdown Table Breaker:** Notice that **all magnitude bars $\vert \cdot \vert$ and conditionals like $\vert H\vert^{2}$ use `\vert`**, never the raw pipe character. This is because KTU exam answer sheets in 2024 scheme frequently use the same character for absolute value as for cell separators in tables, and a stray pipe would break the rendering in many online submission portals.

### 2.4 Decomposition Insight: Inverse × Spectral Correction

Equation 6 in the cheat sheet is the most **pedagogically powerful** form:

$$W(u,v) = \underbrace{\frac{1}{H(u,v)}}_{\text{Inverse Filter}} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{\vert H(u,v)\vert^{2}}{\vert H(u,v)\vert^{2} + K}}_{\text{Spectral Correction Factor} \in [0,1]}$$

The **spectral correction factor** is always between 0 and 1. It:
- Equals $\approx 1$ at frequencies where $\vert H\vert^{2} \gg K$ (we trust the inverse operation).
- Equals $\approx 0$ at frequencies where $\vert H\vert^{2} \ll K$ (we suppress the noisy inverse).

This is the **mathematical heart** of why the Wiener filter is well-behaved even when the inverse filter is unstable.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The MMSE Derivation (Exhaustive, Board-Exam Style)

**Step 1 — Frequency-domain degradation model.**
Fourier transforming the spatial degradation equation:

$$g(x,y) = f(x,y) \ast h(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$$

Yields:

$$G(u,v) = H(u,v)F(u,v) + N(u,v)$$

**Reason:** Convolution in space becomes multiplication in frequency, and convolution is commutative, so $\mathcal{F}\{f \ast h\} = F \cdot H$.

---

**Step 2 — Restrict the search to linear filters.**
We seek a linear filter $W(u,v)$ such that the restored image is:

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = W(u,v)\,G(u,v)$$

**Reason:** For jointly Gaussian (image + noise) processes, the **orthogonality principle** guarantees the MMSE estimator is linear. The image and noise in medical imaging are well-approximated as Gaussian locally, so this restriction is justified.

---

**Step 3 — Define the frequency-domain error.**

$$E(u,v) = F(u,v) - \hat{F}(u,v)$$

---

**Step 4 — Define the Mean-Squared Error objective function.**

$$J(W) = E\{\vert E(u,v)\vert^{2}\} = E\{\vert F(u,v) - W(u,v)G(u,v)\vert^{2}\}$$

We minimize $J$ with respect to $W$.

---

**Step 5 — Expand the squared modulus.**

$$J = E\{[F - WG][F - WG]^{*}\}$$

$$= E\{(F - WG)(F^{*} - W^{*}G^{*})\}$$

$$= E\{FF^{*}\} - W E\{F G^{*}\} - W^{*} E\{G F^{*}\} + \vert W\vert^{2} E\{GG^{*}\}$$

---

**Step 6 — Identify the auto and cross power spectral densities.**

By the **Wiener–Khinchin theorem**, $E\{F(u,v)F^{*}(u,v)\} = S_{ff}(u,v)$, $E\{G(u,v)G^{*}(u,v)\} = S_{gg}(u,v)$, and $E\{F(u,v)G^{*}(u,v)\} = S_{fg}(u,v)$.

So:

$$J = S_{ff} - W S_{fg} - W^{*} S_{gf} + \vert W\vert^{2} S_{gg}$$

---

**Step 7 — Differentiate $J$ with respect to $W^{*}$ and set to zero.**

Since $W$ is treated as a complex variable, we differentiate with respect to $W^{*}$ (or treat $W$ and $W^{*}$ as independent):

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial W^{*}} = -S_{fg} + W\,S_{gg} = 0$$

This gives:

$$W(u,v) = \frac{S_{fg}(u,v)}{S_{gg}(u,v)}$$

**Reason:** The optimal MMSE filter is simply the **cross-PSD divided by the auto-PSD of the observation**. This is the most general Wiener solution.

---

**Step 8 — Express $S_{fg}$ and $S_{gg}$ in terms of $S_{ff}$ and $S_{\eta\eta}$.**

Using $G = HF + N$:

$$S_{gg} = E\{GG^{*}\} = E\{(HF + N)(HF + N)^{*}\}$$

$$= \vert H\vert^{2}E\{FF^{*}\} + H E\{F N^{*}\} + H^{*} E\{N F^{*}\} + E\{NN^{*}\}$$

By assumption 3 (noise uncorrelated with image), $E\{FN^{*}\} = E\{NF^{*}\} = 0$. Therefore:

$$S_{gg} = \vert H\vert^{2} S_{ff} + S_{\eta\eta}$$

Similarly:

$$S_{fg} = E\{F G^{*}\} = E\{F(HF + N)^{*}\} = H^{*} E\{FF^{*}\} + E\{FN^{*}\} = H^{*} S_{ff}$$

---

**Step 9 — Substitute into the optimal $W$.**

$$W(u,v) = \frac{H^{*}(u,v)\,S_{ff}(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v)\vert^{2}\,S_{ff}(u,v) + S_{\eta\eta}(u,v)}$$

---

**Step 10 — Factor out $S_{ff}$ from the denominator.**

$$W(u,v) = \frac{H^{*}(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v)\vert^{2} + \dfrac{S_{\eta\eta}(u,v)}{S_{ff}(u,v)}}$$

Defining the NSR $K(u,v) = S_{\eta\eta}/S_{ff}$:

$$\boxed{\,W(u,v) = \frac{H^{*}(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v)\vert^{2} + K(u,v)}\,}$$

This is the **full Wiener filter transfer function**, the central result of this module.

---

**Step 11 — The white-noise simplification.**
When noise is **white** (flat spectrum), $S_{\eta\eta}(u,v) = \eta_{n}$ (a constant), and the filter reduces to the simpler practical form used in most medical-imaging software libraries:

$$W(u,v) = \frac{1}{H(u,v)} \cdot \frac{\vert H(u,v)\vert^{2}}{\vert H(u,v)\vert^{2} + K}$$

with $K$ now a single scalar tuning parameter.

---

**Step 12 — Minimum achievable MSE.**
Plugging $W$ back into $J$:

$$J_{\min} = S_{ff} - W S_{fg} = S_{ff} - \frac{\vert H\vert^{2} S_{ff}^{2}}{\vert H\vert^{2} S_{ff} + S_{\eta\eta}} = \frac{S_{ff}\,S_{\eta\eta}}{\vert H\vert^{2} S_{ff} + S_{\eta\eta}}$$

**Physical interpretation:** The residual MSE is **directly proportional to the noise PSD** and **inversely proportional to the signal PSD weighted by the blur** — a clean and intuitive engineering result.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Board Exam Style)

> **Problem:** A 2×2 medical ultrasound image patch has a known degradation $H(u,v) = (0.4 + 0.1j)$ at the frequency $(u,v)=(0,0)$. The original-image PSD is $S_{ff} = 16$ and the noise PSD is $S_{\eta\eta} = 4$. Compute the Wiener filter value $W(0,0)$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute $\vert H\vert^{2}$:

$$\vert H(0,0)\vert^{2} = (0.4)^{2} + (0.1)^{2} = 0.16 + 0.01 = 0.17$$

Step 2 — Compute the complex conjugate $H^{*}$:

$$H^{*}(0,0) = 0.4 - 0.1j$$

Step 3 — Compute the NSR $K$:

$$K(0,0) = \frac{S_{\eta\eta}}{S_{ff}} = \frac{4}{16} = 0.25$$

Step 4 — Substitute into the Wiener formula:

$$W(0,0) = \frac{0.4 - 0.1j}{0.17 + 0.25} = \frac{0.4 - 0.1j}{0.42}$$

Step 5 — Final numerical evaluation:

$$W(0,0) = 0.9524 - 0.2381\,j$$

**Verification:** Magnitude is $\sqrt{0.9524^{2} + 0.2381^{2}} \approx 0.982$, which is < 1 — consistent with a noise-suppressing filter. If we had used the pure inverse filter $1/H = (0.4 - 0.1j)/0.17 \approx 2.35 - 0.59j$ (magnitude 2.42), we would have *amplified* noise by a factor of 2.42 — exactly the problem the Wiener filter is designed to fix.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Wiener Filter Operational Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Degraded Medical Image g(x,y)"] --> B["2D FFT Block"]
    B --> C["G(u,v) in Frequency Domain"]
    C --> D["Complex Multiplier Core"]
    E["Known Blur H(u,v) from PSF Estimation"] --> F["Conjugate Operator H Star u v"]
    F --> D
    G["Noise PSD S_eta_eta u v"] --> H["NSR Estimator Block K equals S_eta_eta over S_ff"]
    I["Original Image PSD S_ff u v"] --> H
    H --> D
    D --> J["Restored Spectrum Fhat u v"]
    J --> K["2D IFFT Block"]
    K --> L["Restored Image fhat x y"]
    M["Quality Monitor MSE PSNR SSIM"] --> L
    L --> M
    style A fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#9b1c4d,color:#000
    style L fill:#d8f3dc,stroke:#1b4332,color:#000
    style D fill:#cce3f5,stroke:#0b3d91,color:#000
    style H fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#7a4f01,color:#000
    style M fill:#e0c3fc,stroke:#3c096c,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Adaptive Behavior of Wiener Filter

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Start: Input Frequency Bin u v"] --> B["Compute Mag H squared at u v"]
    B --> C{"Compare with K u v"}
    C -->|"Mag H squared much greater than K"| D["Wiener approaches Inverse Filter 1 over H"]
    C -->|"Mag H squared much less than K"| E["Wiener approaches Smoothed Low Pass Suppressor"]
    C -->|"Mag H squared comparable to K"| F["Wiener Performs Balanced Restoration"]
    D --> G["Output: Aggressive Deblurring"]
    E --> H["Output: Strong Noise Suppression"]
    F --> I["Output: Optimal MMSE Estimate"]
    G --> J["Combine All Frequency Bins via IFFT"]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K["Final Restored Medical Image"]
    style C fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#7a4f01,color:#000
    style K fill:#d8f3dc,stroke:#1b4332,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Wiener Filter Position in the Medical Image Restoration Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1["Patient Body"] --> P2["Scanning Device CT MRI US"]
    P2 --> P3["Raw Sinogram or K Space Data"]
    P3 --> P4["Reconstruction Block FBP or Inverse Radon"]
    P4 --> P5["Degraded Image g x y with Blur and Noise"]
    P5 --> P6["PSF Estimation h x y via Blind Deconvolution"]
    P5 --> P7["Noise Estimation from Image Flat Regions"]
    P6 --> P8["Wiener Filter Engine"]
    P7 --> P8
    P8 --> P9["Restored Image fhat x y"]
    P9 --> P10["Diagnostic Decision by Radiologist"]
    style P1 fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#7a4f01,color:#000
    style P5 fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#9b1c4d,color:#000
    style P8 fill:#cce3f5,stroke:#0b3d91,color:#000
    style P9 fill:#d8f3dc,stroke:#1b4332,color:#000
    style P10 fill:#e0c3fc,stroke:#3c096c,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide:** The blue block (`Wiener Filter Engine`) is the heart of the restoration pipeline. It accepts three inputs: the degraded spectrum $G(u,v)$, the estimated degradation $H(u,v)$, and the estimated NSR $K$. The orange "Raw Sinogram" stage shows that in CT, the Wiener filter can also be applied *before* the inverse Radon transform — a KTU-favorite advanced question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** Define the Wiener filter. State the two fundamental assumptions made during its derivation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Wiener filter** is an optimal linear filter that produces the *minimum mean square error (MMSE)* estimate of an original image from its degraded and noisy version. It is given by
>
> $$W(u,v) = \frac{H^{*}(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v)\vert^{2} + K(u,v)}$$
>
> The two fundamental assumptions are:
>
> 1. **Noise is zero-mean** and **statistically uncorrelated** with the original image.
> 2. The image and noise are **wide-sense stationary (WSS)**, i.e., their mean and autocorrelation are spatially invariant.

---

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2.** What is the Noise-to-Signal Power Ratio (NSR) in the Wiener filter? What is its significance?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **NSR** is defined as
>
> $$K(u,v) = \frac{S_{\eta\eta}(u,v)}{S_{ff}(u,v)} = \frac{\vert N(u,v)\vert^{2}}{\vert F(u,v)\vert^{2}}$$
>
> **Significance:**
> - It controls the **trade-off between deblurring and noise suppression**.
> - When $K$ is **large** (high noise) the filter behaves as a low-pass smoother.
> - When $K$ is **small** (low noise) the filter approaches the pure inverse filter.
> - It can be **empirically estimated** from a flat patch of the image, making the Wiener filter practically implementable even without exact PSD knowledge.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Format)

---

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the frequency-domain transfer function of the Wiener filter starting from the degradation model $g(x,y) = f(x,y) \ast h(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable block diagram, explain the operation of the Wiener filter in the restoration of a blurred and noisy MRI brain image. Discuss how the choice of $K$ affects the result. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution**

**(a) Full Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Degradation model.**
Starting point is the spatial convolution plus additive noise:

$$g(x,y) = f(x,y) \ast h(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$$

Taking the 2D Fourier transform of both sides:

$$G(u,v) = H(u,v)F(u,v) + N(u,v) \quad \text{\dots (1 mark)}$$

**Step 2 — Assumptions.**
(i) Image and noise are **wide-sense stationary**.
(ii) Noise has **zero mean**.
(iii) Noise is **uncorrelated** with the original image.
