### Stage A: Splitting Phase (Top-Down)

The algorithm begins with the **entire image as a single region** $R_0$ and recursively partitions it:

1. **Initialize**: Treat the whole image as the root node of a quadtree.
2. **Test Predicate**: Evaluate $P(R)$ for the current region.
3. **Decision Branch**:
   - If $P(R) = \text{TRUE}$ (homogeneous) → mark as **leaf node**, stop.
   - If $P(R) = \text{FALSE}$ (non-homogeneous) → **split** $R$ into 4 equal quadrants:
     $$R \rightarrow \{R_{\text{NW}}, R_{\text{NE}}, R_{\text{SW}}, R_{\text{SE}}\}$$
4. **Recursion**: Apply step 2–3 to each new quadrant until all nodes are leaves.

### Stage B: Merging Phase (Bottom-Up)

After splitting, the quadtree is traversed to merge adjacent leaves:

1. **Adjacency Test**: For any two neighboring regions $R_i$ and $R_j$:
   - 4-adjacency (sharing an edge), or
   - 8-adjacency (sharing an edge or corner).
2. **Combined Predicate**: If $P(R_i \cup R_j) = \text{TRUE}$ → merge.
3. **Iterate**: Repeat until no further merges are possible.

### Stage C: Combined Split-and-Merge Algorithm

The most common implementation **interleaves** splitting and merging to avoid over-fragmentation:

- After every split, check if the new sub-regions should merge with neighbors.
- After every merge, check if the resulting region is now non-uniform (re-split if needed).

## 2.2 Quadtree Representation

A quadtree is a tree where each internal node has exactly **4 children**. For an $N \times N$ image (where $N = 2^k$), the maximum depth is $k$ and the maximum number of nodes is:

$$N_{\text{nodes}} = \sum_{i=0}^{k} 4^i = \frac{4^{k+1} - 1}{3}$$

For a $512 \times 512$ image ($k=9$): $N_{\text{nodes}} = \frac{4^{10}-1}{3} = \frac{1{,}048{,}575}{3} = 349{,}525$ nodes (worst case).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units / Threshold |
|---|-------------------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $P(R) = \text{TRUE}$ if homogeneous | Predicate function on region $R$ | Boolean |
| 2 | $\max(R) - \min(R) \leq T$ | Intensity range predicate | $T$ in gray levels (0–255) |
| 3 | $\sigma^2(R) = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(g_i - \mu)^2$ | Region variance predicate | $T_\sigma$ in $(\text{GL})^2$ |
| 4 | $\mu(R) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} g_i$ | Mean intensity of region $R$ | Gray levels |
| 5 | $\vert \mu(R_i) - \mu(R_j) \vert \leq T_\mu$ | Merging predicate between regions | $T_\mu$ in gray levels |
| 6 | $N_{\text{nodes}}^{\max} = \frac{4^{k+1}-1}{3}$ | Max quadtree nodes for $2^k \times 2^k$ image | Dimensionless |
| 7 | Quadtree depth | Max depth = $k = \log_2 N$ | Levels |
| 8 | Adjacency | 4-connectivity (edge) or 8-connectivity (edge+corner) | Pixels |
| 9 | $R \to \{R_{\text{NW}}, R_{\text{NE}}, R_{\text{SW}}, R_{\text{SE}}\}$ | 4-way quadrant split | Equal quadrants |
| 10 | Termination | $\forall R_i, P(R_i) = \text{TRUE}$ **and** no merge possible | Both conditions |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pipeline reminder:** Splitting alone leads to **over-segmentation** (neighboring identical regions remain separate). Merging alone (region growing from seeds) is sensitive to **seed selection** and **order**. The hybrid approach overcomes both weaknesses.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **Medical Imaging** | Brain tumor isolation in MRI, organ delineation in CT |
| **Remote Sensing** | Land-cover classification in satellite imagery |
| **Document Analysis** | Text/background separation in scanned documents |
| **Industrial Inspection** | Defect detection in manufactured surfaces |
| **Microscopy** | Cell counting and nuclear segmentation in histopathology |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Region Variance Predicate

The variance of a region $R$ containing $n$ pixels with intensities $\{g_1, g_2, \ldots, g_n\}$ measures the **spread** of intensity values. A homogeneous region has low variance.

**Step 1 — Mean Calculation**

Compute the mean intensity of the region:

$$\mu(R) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} g_i$$

**Step 2 — Squared Deviations**

For each pixel, compute the deviation from the mean and square it:

$$d_i = g_i - \mu(R)$$

$$d_i^2 = (g_i - \mu(R))^2$$

**Step 3 — Sum of Squared Deviations**

Add up all squared deviations:

$$S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (g_i - \mu(R))^2$$

**Step 4 — Variance**

Normalize by the number of pixels (using $n-1$ for sample variance, or $n$ for population variance):

$$\sigma^2(R) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(g_i - \mu(R))^2$$

**Step 5 — Homogeneity Decision**

The region is declared homogeneous (and splitting stops) if:

$$\sigma^2(R) \leq T_\sigma$$

where $T_\sigma$ is a user-defined threshold (typical values: 50–500 for 8-bit images).

## 3.2 Derivation: Intensity Range Predicate (Faster Alternative)

The variance computation is $O(n)$ per region. A faster $O(1)$ check uses the range:

$$\text{Range}(R) = \max_{i \in R}(g_i) - \min_{i \in R}(g_i)$$

$$\text{Homogeneous} \iff \text{Range}(R) \leq T_r$$

This requires only two passes (one for max, one for min) regardless of region size.

## 3.3 Worked Example: $4 \times 4$ Image Splitting

Consider a $4 \times 4$ image (so $k=2$, quadtree has max depth 2) with intensity values:

$$
I = \begin{bmatrix}
10 & 12 & 200 & 205 \\
11 & 13 & 198 & 202 \\
9 & 14 & 50 & 55 \\
8 & 15 & 48 & 52
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Threshold**: $T_r = 20$ (range predicate).

**Step 1 — Test the entire image $R_0$**

- $\max(R_0) = 205$
- $\min(R_0) = 8$
- $\text{Range}(R_0) = 205 - 8 = 197$
- $197 > 20$ → **Split $R_0$ into 4 quadrants.**

**Step 2 — Test Top-Left Quadrant $R_1$**

$$R_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 12 \\ 11 & 13 \end{bmatrix}$$

- $\max(R_1) = 13$, $\min(R_1) = 10$
- $\text{Range}(R_1) = 3 \leq 20$ → **Homogeneous (leaf).**

**Step 3 — Test Top-Right Quadrant $R_2$**

$$R_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 200 & 205 \\ 198 & 202 \end{bmatrix}$$

- $\max(R_2) = 205$, $\min(R_2) = 198$
- $\text{Range}(R_2) = 7 \leq 20$ → **Homogeneous (leaf).**

**Step 4 — Test Bottom-Left Quadrant $R_3$**

$$R_3 = \begin{bmatrix} 9 & 14 \\ 8 & 15 \end{bmatrix}$$

- $\max(R_3) = 15$, $\min(R_3) = 8$
- $\text{Range}(R_3) = 7 \leq 20$ → **Homogeneous (leaf).**

**Step 5 — Test Bottom-Right Quadrant $R_4$**

$$R_4 = \begin{bmatrix} 50 & 55 \\ 48 & 52 \end{bmatrix}$$

- $\max(R_4) = 55$, $\min(R_4) = 48$
- $\text{Range}(R_4) = 7 \leq 20$ → **Homogeneous (leaf).**

**Step 6 — Merging Phase**

Check adjacency:
- $R_1$ (top-left) ∪ $R_3$ (bottom-left): range of combined region = $15 - 8 = 7 \leq 20$ → **Merge** into $M_1$.
- $R_2$ (top-right) ∪ $R_4$ (bottom-right): range = $205 - 48 = 157 > 20$ → **No merge**.
- $R_1$ ∪ $R_2$: range = $205 - 8 = 197 > 20$ → **No merge**.

**Final Segmentation**:
- Region $M_1$ = entire left half (8 pixels).
- Region $R_2$ = top-right quadrant (4 pixels).
- Region $R_4$ = bottom-right quadrant (4 pixels).

**Result**: 3 distinct regions, corresponding to a "left background," "bright top-right," and "mid-intensity bottom-right."

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Pure Splitting | Pure Merging (Region Growing) | Split + Merge (Hybrid) |
|--------|----------------|-------------------------------|------------------------|
| Direction | Top-down | Bottom-up | Both |
| Data Structure | Quadtree | Adjacency graph | Quadtree + merge list |
| Seed Required? | No | Yes | No |
| Sensitive to Order? | No | Yes | No |
| Over-segmentation? | High (adjacent similar regions) | Low | Low |
| Under-segmentation? | Low | Possible (early merging) | Low |
| Computational Cost | Moderate | High for large seeds | Moderate |
| Closed Boundaries? | Yes | Depends on seeds | Yes |
| KTU Preference | Mention but critique | Compare | **Primary answer** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Split-and-Merge Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Full Image R0] --> B{Evaluate P R0}
    B -- TRUE --> C[Mark as Leaf Region]
    B -- FALSE --> D[Split R0 into 4 Quadrants]
    D --> E[NW Quadrant] 
    D --> F[NE Quadrant]
    D --> G[SW Quadrant]
    D --> H[SE Quadrant]
    E --> I{Evaluate P R1}
    F --> J{Evaluate P R2}
    G --> K{Evaluate P R3}
    H --> L{Evaluate P R4}
    I -- TRUE --> M[Leaf R1]
    I -- FALSE --> N[Recurse Split R1]
    J -- TRUE --> O[Leaf R2]
    J -- FALSE --> P[Recurse Split R2]
    K -- TRUE --> Q[Leaf R3]
    K -- FALSE --> R[Recurse Split R3]
    L -- TRUE --> S[Leaf R4]
    L -- FALSE --> T[Recurse Split R4]
    M --> U[Merge Phase: Check Adjacent Leaf Pairs]
    O --> U
    Q --> U
    S --> U
    N --> U
    P --> U
    R --> U
    T --> U
    U --> V{Any Pair Satisfies Combined Predicate}
    V -- YES --> W[Merge the Pair]
    W --> U
    V -- NO --> X[Final Segmentation Output]
```

## 4.2 Quadtree Structure for the $4 \times 4$ Worked Example

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT["Root: Full Image Range = 197"]
    ROOT --> NW["NW: R1 Range = 3 LEAF"]
    ROOT --> NE["NE: R2 Range = 7 LEAF"]
    ROOT --> SW["SW: R3 Range = 7 LEAF"]
    ROOT --> SE["SE: R4 Range = 7 LEAF"]
    SW -.Merge.- M1["Merged Region M1: Left Half Range = 7"]
    NW -.Merge.- M1
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Pipeline View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IMG[Input Medical Image] --> PRED[Homogeneity Predicate Module]
    PRED --> SPLIT[Splitting Engine: Quadtree Partition]
    SPLIT --> QUAD[Quadtree Leaf Set]
    QUAD --> ADJ[Adjacency Builder]
    ADJ --> MERGE[Merging Engine: Predicate on Pairs]
    MERGE --> OUT[Segmented Region Map]
    OUT --> VIS[Visualization and Overlay]
    OUT --> MORPH[Optional Morphological Refinement]
    MORPH --> FINAL[Final Region Label Image]
```

## 4.4 Predicate Selection Decision Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Choose Homogeneity Predicate] --> Q1{Is speed critical}
    Q1 -- YES --> Q2[Use Intensity Range: max minus min]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q3{Is texture important}
    Q3 -- YES --> Q4[Use Variance: sigma squared test]
    Q3 -- NO --> Q5{Need statistical robustness}
    Q5 -- YES --> Q6[Use Standard Deviation with t test]
    Q5 -- NO --> Q7[Use Simple Mean Comparison]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define region splitting and merging in image segmentation. State the role of the homogeneity predicate $P(R)$.**

**Model Answer:**

Region splitting and merging is a hybrid region-based segmentation technique. In the **splitting phase**, a region $R$ that does not satisfy the homogeneity predicate $P(R)$ is subdivided into four equal quadrants (NW, NE, SW, SE), represented as a **quadtree**. The process continues recursively. In the **merging phase**, adjacent regions $R_i$ and $R_j$ are fused if $P(R_i \cup R_j) = \text{TRUE}$.

The **homogeneity predicate $P(R)$** is a Boolean function that determines whether a region is uniform based on a chosen criterion such as intensity range, variance, or mean. It controls the granularity of segmentation and balances over-segmentation versus under-segmentation. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: List any three homogeneity predicates used in region splitting and merging. Mention one advantage and one limitation of the intensity range predicate.**

**Model Answer:**

Three homogeneity predicates:

1. **Intensity Range Predicate**: $\max(R) - \min(R) \leq T_r$
2. **Variance Predicate**: $\sigma^2(R) \leq T_\sigma$
3. **Mean Difference Predicate** (for merging): $\vert \mu(R_i) - \mu(R_j) \vert \leq T_\mu$

**Advantage** of intensity range predicate: Computationally fast, requiring only two passes per region (one for max, one for min).

**Limitation**: It is sensitive to **outlier pixels** (a single salt-and-pepper noise pixel can prevent merging of an otherwise homogeneous region). **(3 marks)**

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Choice-Based)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **splitting phase** of the region splitting and merging algorithm. Describe the quadtree representation and illustrate with a $4 \times 4$ example using the range predicate with threshold $T_r = 20$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **merging phase** with appropriate predicates. Compare region splitting and merging with region growing. State two medical imaging applications.

#### Model Solution for (a)

The **splitting phase** works top-down:

1. Start with the entire image $R_0$ as the root of a quadtree.
2. Evaluate the predicate $P(R_0)$. If TRUE, stop. If FALSE, split $R_0$ into 4 equal quadrants.
3. Recursively apply step 2 to each quadrant until all regions satisfy $P(R)$.

**Quadtree Representation**: Each internal node represents a region that was split, and each leaf represents a final homogeneous region. For an $N \times N$ image with $N = 2^k$, maximum depth is $k$.

**Illustration with $4 \times 4$ Example**:

Image:
$$
I = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 12 & 200 & 205 \\ 11 & 13 & 198 & 202 \\ 9 & 14 & 50 & 55 \\ 8 & 15 & 48 & 52 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Threshold $T_r = 20$.

| Region | Max | Min | Range | Decision |
|--------|-----|-----|-------|----------|
| Full image | 205 | 8 | 197 | Split |
| NW (top-left 2x2) | 13 | 10 | 3 | Leaf |
| NE (top-right 2x2) | 205 | 198 | 7 | Leaf |
| SW (bottom-left 2x2) | 15 | 8 | 7 | Leaf |
| SE (bottom-right 2x2) | 55 | 48 | 7 | Leaf |

Result: 4 leaf regions after splitting. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Merging Phase**: After splitting, scan all adjacent leaf pairs $(R_i, R_j)$ and test the combined predicate $P(R_i \cup R_j)$. If TRUE, merge them. Continue until no further merges are possible.

Common merging predicates:
- $\vert \mu(R_i) - \mu(R_j) \vert \leq T_\mu$
- Combined range: $\max(R_i \cup R_j) - \min(R_i \cup R_j) \leq T_r$

**Comparison with Region Growing**:

| Aspect | Split-Merge | Region Growing |
|--------|-------------|----------------|
| Seed | Not required | Required |
| Order sensitivity | None | High |
| Starting point | Whole image | Seed pixels |
| Closed boundaries | Yes | Depends |

**Medical Applications**:
1. **Brain MRI segmentation** — separating gray matter, white matter, and CSF.
2. **Tumor delineation in CT scans** — isolating tumor mass from surrounding healthy tissue. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **variance-based homogeneity predicate** for region splitting. Apply it to determine if the following $3 \times 3$ region is homogeneous with threshold $T_\sigma = 50$:

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix} 100 & 102 & 98 \\ 101 & 99 & 103 \\ 97 & 100 & 100 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **combined split-and-merge algorithm**. State the termination condition and discuss computational complexity in terms of image size $N \times N$.

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Derivation**:

The mean intensity:

$$\mu(R) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} g_i$$

The variance:

$$\sigma^2(R) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(g_i - \mu(R))^2$$

The homogeneity test:

$$P(R) = \text{TRUE} \iff \sigma^2(R) \leq T_\sigma$$

**Application**:

Sum of intensities: $100 + 102 + 98 + 101 + 99 + 103 + 97 + 100 + 100 = 900$. Number of pixels $n = 9$.

Mean:

$$\mu = \frac{900}{9} = 100$$

Squared deviations:

$$\begin{aligned}
(100-100)^2 &= 0 \\
(102-100)^2 &= 4 \\
(98-100)^2 &= 4 \\
(101-100)^2 &= 1 \\
(99-100)^2 &= 1 \\
(103-100)^2 &= 9 \\
(97-100)^2 &= 9 \\
(100-100)^2 &= 0 \\
(100-100)^2 &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

Sum of squared deviations: $0 + 4 + 4 + 1 + 1 + 9 + 9 + 0 + 0 = 28$.

Variance:

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{28}{9} \approx 3.11$$

Decision: $3.11 \leq 50$ → **Homogeneous (leaf, do not split)**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Combined Split-and-Merge Algorithm**:

1. If $P(R) = \text{FALSE}$, split $R$ into 4 quadrants.
2. After splitting, for every adjacent pair of quadrants, if $P(R_i \cup R_j) = \text{TRUE}$, merge them.
3. If a merged region now violates $P(R)$, split it again.
4. Repeat steps 1–3 until convergence.

**Termination Condition**:

$$\forall R_i: P(R_i) = \text{TRUE} \quad \text{AND} \quad \forall (R_i, R_j) \text{ adjacent}: P(R_i \cup R_j) = \text{FALSE}$$

**Computational Complexity**:

For an $N \times N$ image ($N = 2^k$):
- Quadtree has at most $\frac{4^{k+1}-1}{3} = O(N^2)$ nodes.
- Splitting each node: $O(1)$ to $O(n)$ depending on predicate (range = $O(1)$, variance = $O(n)$).
- Total worst case: $O(N^2)$ for range predicate, $O(N^2 \log N)$ to $O(N^3)$ for variance predicate over all nodes.
- Merging: $O(N^2)$ adjacency checks in the worst case.
- **Practical complexity**: $O(N^2)$ for the range predicate, which is the most common choice. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the predicate definition**: Students often describe the algorithm without writing $P(R)$ explicitly. Always state the predicate and threshold.
> 2. **Confusing splitting and merging directions**: Splitting is **top-down** (whole → parts); merging is **bottom-up** (parts → whole). Marks are lost when these are swapped.
> 3. **Skipping the quadtree**: KTU examiners expect the **quadtree data structure** to be mentioned explicitly with maximum depth formula.
> 4. **No derivation in long answers**: For 14-mark questions, simply stating the variance formula is insufficient — you must show the **step-by-step computation** with squared deviations.
> 5. **Missing medical context**: For a course on Advanced Medical Image Processing, every answer should ideally cite at least one medical application (MRI, CT, tumor segmentation).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Region splitting and merging** is a **hybrid, quadtree-based** segmentation technique combining top-down splitting and bottom-up merging.
- The **homogeneity predicate $P(R)$** is the decision function; common choices are **intensity range, variance, and mean difference**.
- **Splitting**: $P(R) = \text{FALSE}$ → divide $R$ into 4 equal quadrants; repeat until homogeneous.
- **Merging**: Adjacent $R_i, R_j$ with $P(R_i \cup R_j) = \text{TRUE}$ → combine; repeat until no more merges.
- The **quadtree** is a 4-ary tree; for a $2^k \times 2^k$ image, maximum depth is $k$ and maximum nodes is $\frac{4^{k+1}-1}{3}$.
- **Quadtree nodes**: Internal = split regions; Leaf = final homogeneous regions.
- **Termination**: All regions satisfy $P$ **AND** no adjacent pair can be merged.
- **Range predicate** is fastest (two passes); **variance predicate** is more robust to outliers but costlier ($O(n)$ per region).
- **Advantages**: Closed boundaries, no seed required, order-independent, multi-resolution.
- **Limitations**: Blocky boundaries (since splits are always into 4 equal squares), threshold sensitivity.
- **Comparison**: Region growing needs seeds and is order-sensitive; splitting alone causes over-segmentation; the hybrid approach overcomes both.
- **Medical applications**: Brain tissue segmentation in MRI, tumor delineation in CT, organ isolation in ultrasound, cell segmentation in histopathology.
- **Key formula to memorize**: $\sigma^2(R) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(g_i - \mu)^2 \leq T_\sigma$.
